Given a design matrix \( X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d} \) (where each row is a sample and a column of ones is prepended for the bias term) and a target vector \( \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^n \), the linear model predicts \( \hat{\mathbf{y}} = X\boldsymbol{\beta} \). The OLS objective minimizes the squared error:

\[ J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \| \mathbf{y} - X\boldsymbol{\beta} \|_2^2 = (\mathbf{y} - X\boldsymbol{\beta})^\top (\mathbf{y} - X\boldsymbol{\beta}) \]

Setting \( \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} J = 0 \) yields the normal equations:

\[ X^\top X \boldsymbol{\beta} = X^\top \mathbf{y} \]

When \( X^\top X \) is invertible (i.e., the columns of \( X \) are linearly independent), the unique solution is:

\[ \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y} \]

The matrix \( X^+ = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \) is the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse of \( X \).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Geometric intuition: The OLS prediction \(X\beta^*\) is the orthogonal projection of \(y\) onto the column space of \(X\). The residual vector is orthogonal to all columns of \(X\).

Computational cost. Forming \( X^\top X \) costs \( O(nd^2) \) and inverting it costs \( O(d^3) \). This is efficient when \( d \) is small, but for large feature counts gradient descent or QR/SVD decompositions are preferred. Ridge regression modifies the solution to \( \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X^\top X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y} \), which guarantees invertibility and acts as L2 regularization.

Statistical properties. Under the Gauss–Markov assumptions (linear model, zero-mean homoscedastic uncorrelated errors), the OLS estimator is the Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE) — it has the lowest variance among all linear unbiased estimators.

Derivations

Expand the squared error:

\[ J = \mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top X^\top \mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top X^\top X \boldsymbol{\beta} \]

Take the gradient with respect to \( \boldsymbol{\beta} \) using standard matrix calculus identities:

\[ \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} J = -2 X^\top \mathbf{y} + 2 X^\top X \boldsymbol{\beta} \]

Setting this to zero:

\[ X^\top X \boldsymbol{\beta} = X^\top \mathbf{y} \implies \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y} \]

The Hessian is \( 2X^\top X \), which is positive semi-definite, confirming this is a minimum.

Python 
import numpy as np

# Direct solution (educational, not numerically stable)
beta = np.linalg.inv(X.T @ X) @ X.T @ y

# Preferred: use lstsq which uses SVD internally
beta, residuals, rank, sv = np.linalg.lstsq(X, y, rcond=None)

title: PAC Learning (Probably Approximately Correct)
summary: A rigorous mathematical framework that formalizes when and how efficiently a learning algorithm can identify a hypothesis that is "approximately correct" with "high probability." PAC learning provides the theoretical foundation for sampl…

papers.citation: 1984 · Leslie Valiant · A Theory of the Learnable

sections.body:
A rigorous mathematical framework that formalizes when and how efficiently a learning algorithm can identify a hypothesis that is "approximately correct" with "high probability." PAC learning provides the theoretical foundation for sample complexity bounds and computational learning theory.

A concept class \( \mathcal{C} \) is PAC-learnable by a hypothesis class \( \mathcal{H} \) if there exists an algorithm \( A \) such that for every target concept \( c \in \mathcal{C} \), every distribution \( \mathcal{D} \) over the input space, and every \( \varepsilon, \delta \in (0, 1) \):

Given \( m \) i.i.d. samples drawn from \( \mathcal{D} \), algorithm \( A \) outputs a hypothesis \( h \in \mathcal{H} \) satisfying:

\[ \Pr\bigl[\text{err}_{\mathcal{D}}(h) \le \varepsilon\bigr] \ge 1 - \delta \]

where the generalization error is \( \text{err}_{\mathcal{D}}(h) = \Pr_{x \sim \mathcal{D}}[h(x) \ne c(x)] \). The key requirement is that the sample complexity \( m \) must be polynomial in \( 1/\varepsilon \), \( 1/\delta \), and the size of the representation.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: PAC ensures the hypothesis space mostly overlaps with the concept (Error \( \le \varepsilon \)) with high confidence (\( \ge 1-\delta \)).

Sample complexity bound (finite hypothesis class). For a finite hypothesis class \( |\mathcal{H}| \), a consistent learner (one that perfectly fits the training data) achieves generalization error \( \le \varepsilon \) with probability \( \ge 1 - \delta \) if:

\[ m \ge \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\left(\ln|\mathcal{H}| + \ln\frac{1}{\delta}\right) \]

For infinite hypothesis classes, the VC dimension \( d_{\text{VC}} \) replaces \( \ln|\mathcal{H}| \) as the measure of capacity:

\[ m = O\!\left(\frac{d_{\text{VC}} + \ln(1/\delta)}{\varepsilon^2}\right) \]

sections.title: Key parameters
sections.body:
• \( \varepsilon \) (accuracy): upper bound on generalization error — smaller means more accurate
  • \( \delta \) (confidence): probability of failure — smaller means more reliable

Connections. PAC learning motivates structural risk minimization and regularization: controlling hypothesis complexity (VC dimension) prevents overfitting. The framework also underpins the theoretical justification for SVMs, which minimize an upper bound on generalization error.

title: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) & AUC
summary: The ROC curve plots a binary classifier's True Positive Rate against its False Positive Rate across all decision thresholds, providing a threshold-independent view of discriminative performance. The Area Under the Curve (AUC) compresses…

papers.citation: 1941–1950s · Signal detection theory (Peterson, Birdsall, Fox) · ML adoption: Swets (1988), Fawcett (2006)

sections.body:
The ROC curve plots a binary classifier's True Positive Rate against its False Positive Rate across all decision thresholds, providing a threshold-independent view of discriminative performance. The Area Under the Curve (AUC) compresses this curve into a single scalar: the probability that the classifier ranks a random positive higher than a random negative.

For a binary classifier with tunable threshold \( \tau \), define:

Metric Formula Synonym
True Positive Rate (TPR) \( \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \) Sensitivity, Recall
False Positive Rate (FPR) \( \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \) 1 − Specificity

The ROC curve is the parametric plot \( \bigl(\text{FPR}(\tau),\, \text{TPR}(\tau)\bigr) \) as \( \tau \) varies from \( +\infty \) to \( -\infty \).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The Area Under the Curve (AUC) is the shaded green area.

sections.title: Key reference points
sections.body:
• Random classifier: the diagonal line \( y = x \), AUC = 0.5
  • Perfect classifier: passes through \( (0, 1) \), AUC = 1.0
  • Worst classifier: AUC < 0.5 (inverted predictions; flipping labels fixes it)

Probabilistic interpretation. The AUC equals the Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney statistic:

\[ \text{AUC} = \Pr(s(x^+) > s(x^-)) \]

where \( s(\cdot) \) is the classifier's score function, \( x^+ \) is a randomly drawn positive, and \( x^- \) a randomly drawn negative.

Strengths and limitations. ROC-AUC is invariant to class prior — it measures ranking quality, not calibration. This makes it suitable for comparing models across different prevalence settings. However, under heavy class imbalance, the Precision–Recall curve (and its AUC, PR-AUC) is often more informative because FPR can remain low even with many false positives when negatives vastly outnumber positives.

Historical note. ROC analysis originated in signal detection theory during World War II, where radar operators needed to distinguish enemy aircraft from noise. It was adopted by the medical diagnostics community in the 1960s and became a standard ML evaluation metric in the 1990s.

title: Hidden Markov Model (HMM)
summary: A generative probabilistic model for sequential data in which the system transitions through a sequence of discrete hidden states according to a Markov chain, and each hidden state emits an observable symbol. HMMs were the dominant appro…

papers.citation: 1966 · Leonard E. Baum et al. · Foundational applications: Rabiner (1989), "A Tutorial on Hidden Markov Models and Selected Applications in Speech Recognition"

sections.body:
A generative probabilistic model for sequential data in which the system transitions through a sequence of discrete hidden states according to a Markov chain, and each hidden state emits an observable symbol. HMMs were the dominant approach in speech recognition and biological sequence analysis before deep learning.

An HMM is fully specified by the tuple \( \lambda = (\boldsymbol{\pi}, A, B) \):

  • \( \boldsymbol{\pi} \in \mathbb{R}^N \): initial state distribution, where \( \pi_i = P(q_1 = i) \)
  • \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N} \): state transition matrix, where \( a_{ij} = P(q_t = j \mid q_{t-1} = i) \)
  • \( B \): emission probabilities, where \( b_j(o_t) = P(o_t \mid q_t = j) \)

Here \( N \) is the number of hidden states, \( q_t \) is the hidden state at time \( t \), and \( o_t \) is the observation at time \( t \). The Markov assumption states that \( q_t \) depends only on \( q_{t-1} \).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The probabilistic graphical model of an HMM unrolled over time.

sections.title: Three fundamental problems
sections.body:
Problem Task Algorithm
Evaluation Compute \( P(O \mid \lambda) \) Forward algorithm
Decoding Find most likely state sequence Viterbi algorithm
Learning Estimate \( \lambda \) from data Baum–Welch (EM)

The forward algorithm computes the likelihood by marginalizing over all possible state sequences using dynamic programming, running in \( O(N^2 T) \) time versus \( O(N^T) \) for brute force. The Viterbi algorithm finds the most probable path through the state trellis using the recursion:

\[ \delta_t(j) = \max_{i} \bigl[\delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\bigr] \cdot b_j(o_t) \]

where \( \delta_t(j) \) is the probability of the most likely path ending in state \( j \) at time \( t \).

Connection to modern models. HMMs can be viewed as a special case of dynamic Bayesian networks. Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) address HMM limitations by modeling \( P(\mathbf{q} \mid \mathbf{o}) \) directly (discriminative) rather than the joint. Sequence-to-sequence neural models and Transformers have largely replaced HMMs in NLP and speech, though HMMs remain useful for their interpretability and exact inference guarantees.

title: Convex Function
summary: A function whose epigraph (the region above its graph) is a convex set, equivalently one for which any chord between two points on the graph lies on or above the graph. Convexity is the single most important structural property in optimi…

papers.citation: Classical mathematics · Modern treatment: Boyd & Vandenberghe, Convex Optimization (2004)

sections.body:
A function whose epigraph (the region above its graph) is a convex set, equivalently one for which any chord between two points on the graph lies on or above the graph. Convexity is the single most important structural property in optimization: it guarantees that every local minimum is a global minimum and that gradient descent converges to it.

Definition. A function \( f : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R} \) is convex if, for all \( \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \) in its domain and all \( t \in [0, 1] \):

\[ f\bigl(t\mathbf{x} + (1-t)\mathbf{y}\bigr) \le t\,f(\mathbf{x}) + (1-t)\,f(\mathbf{y}) \]

If the inequality is strict for \( \mathbf{x} \ne \mathbf{y} \) and \( t \in (0,1) \), the function is strictly convex, which guarantees a unique global minimum.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The chord (secant line segment) between any two points lies above the function's graph.

Second-order characterization. If \( f \) is twice differentiable, it is convex if and only if its Hessian is positive semi-definite everywhere:

\[ \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}) \succeq 0 \quad \forall\, \mathbf{x} \]

For strictly convex: \( \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}) \succ 0 \) (positive definite).

First-order characterization. For differentiable \( f \), convexity is equivalent to the tangent line being a global underestimator:

\[ f(\mathbf{y}) \ge f(\mathbf{x}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x})^\top (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x}) \quad \forall\, \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \]

This is why gradient descent works: the gradient always points toward lower function values, and there are no misleading local minima.

sections.title: Examples in ML
sections.body:
• Convex losses: Mean Squared Error, cross-entropy (w.r.t. linear model outputs), hinge loss, logistic loss
  • Convex regularizers: L1 norm (convex, not strictly), L2 norm squared (strictly convex)
  • Non-convex: deep neural network loss surfaces (composition with nonlinear activations destroys convexity)

Operations preserving convexity: non-negative weighted sums, pointwise maximum, affine composition \( f(Ax + b) \). These rules let us verify convexity of composite objectives without computing Hessians.

title: Shannon Entropy
summary: The fundamental quantity from information theory that measures the expected "surprise" or uncertainty in the outcomes of a random variable. Shannon entropy is the building block from which cross-entropy, KL divergence, and mutual informa…

papers.citation: 1948 · Claude Shannon · A Mathematical Theory of Communication

sections.body:
The fundamental quantity from information theory that measures the expected "surprise" or uncertainty in the outcomes of a random variable. Shannon entropy is the building block from which cross-entropy, KL divergence, and mutual information are constructed, making it central to classification losses, decision trees, and information-theoretic analysis of models.

For a discrete random variable \( X \) with outcomes \( x \in \mathcal{X} \) and probability mass function \( p(x) \), the entropy is:

\[ H(X) = -\sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} p(x) \log p(x) \]

with the convention \( 0 \log 0 = 0 \). If the logarithm base is 2, entropy is measured in bits; if base \( e \), in nats.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The entropy of a binary variable is maximized when the probabilities are equal (maximum uncertainty).

sections.title: Key properties
sections.body:
• Non-negativity: \( H(X) \ge 0 \), with equality iff \( X \) is deterministic (one outcome has probability 1).
  • Maximum entropy: for \( |\mathcal{X}| = k \) outcomes, \( H(X) \le \log k \), achieved by the uniform distribution. This connects to the maximum entropy principle in statistical mechanics and motivates maximum-entropy classifiers.
  • Concavity: \( H \) is a concave function of the distribution \( p \).

Concrete example. A fair coin: \( H = -\tfrac{1}{2}\log_2\tfrac{1}{2} - \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2\tfrac{1}{2} = 1 \) bit. A biased coin with \( p(\text{heads}) = 0.9 \): \( H \approx 0.47 \) bits — less uncertainty because the outcome is more predictable.

sections.title: Related quantities
sections.body:
• Cross-entropy: \( H(p, q) = -\sum p(x) \log q(x) \) — the expected code length when using distribution \( q \) to encode data from \( p \). This is the standard classification loss in neural networks.
  • KL divergence: \( D_{\text{KL}}(p \| q) = H(p, q) - H(p) \ge 0 \) — the extra bits needed due to using \( q \) instead of \( p \).
  • Mutual information: \( I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y) \) — the reduction in uncertainty about \( X \) given knowledge of \( Y \). Used in decision tree splitting (information gain) and feature selection.

title: L1 vs. L2 Norms
summary: Two fundamental vector norms that measure magnitude in different ways. The L1 norm sums absolute values (Manhattan distance) and induces sparsity when used as a penalty; the L2 norm computes the Euclidean length and distributes weight ev…

papers.citation: L1 regression (Lasso): Tibshirani (1996) · L2 regression (Ridge): Hoerl & Kennard (1970) · Elastic Net: Zou & Hastie (2005)

sections.body:
Two fundamental vector norms that measure magnitude in different ways. The L1 norm sums absolute values (Manhattan distance) and induces sparsity when used as a penalty; the L2 norm computes the Euclidean length and distributes weight evenly. Their choice as regularization penalties (Lasso vs. Ridge) has profound effects on model structure.

The general \( L_p \) norm of a vector \( \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \) is:

\[ \|\mathbf{x}\|_p = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i|^p\right)^{1/p} \]

L1 norm (\( p = 1 \), Manhattan distance):

\[ \|\mathbf{x}\|_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i| \]

L2 norm (\( p = 2 \), Euclidean distance):

\[ \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2} \]

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Because the L1 constraint region is a diamond with corners exactly on the axes, the objective function's contour lines are more likely to first intersect there, driving certain weights exactly to zero.

sections.title: As regularization penalties
sections.body:
Property L1 (Lasso) L2 (Ridge)
Penalty term \( \lambda \|\mathbf{w}\|_1 \) \( \lambda \|\mathbf{w}\|_2^2 \)
Effect on weights Drives weights to exactly zero (sparsity) Shrinks weights toward zero uniformly
Feature selection Yes (implicit) No (all features retained)
Differentiability Not differentiable at 0 Smooth everywhere
Gradient / subgradient \( \text{sign}(x_i) \) \( 2x_i \)
Elastic Net combines both: \( \lambda_1 \|\mathbf{w}\|_1 + \lambda_2 \|\mathbf{w}\|_2^2 \), getting sparsity from L1 while maintaining the grouping effect and stability of L2 for correlated features.

title: K-Means Objective Function (Inertia)
summary: The objective that K-Means clustering minimizes: the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS), also called inertia. It measures the total squared Euclidean distance from each data point to its assigned cluster centroid, and its minimization…

papers.citation: 1957 · Stuart Lloyd (published 1982) · K-Means++: Arthur & Vassilvitskii (2007)

sections.body:
The objective that K-Means clustering minimizes: the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS), also called inertia. It measures the total squared Euclidean distance from each data point to its assigned cluster centroid, and its minimization drives the iterative assign–update loop of Lloyd's algorithm.

Given \( n \) observations \( \{\mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_n\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d \) and a desired number of clusters \( k \), K-Means seeks a partition \( S = \{S_1, \dots, S_k\} \) and centroids \( \{\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \dots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_k\} \) that minimize:

\[ J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in S_i} \|\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i\|_2^2 \]

where \( \boldsymbol{\mu}_i = \frac{1}{|S_i|}\sum_{\mathbf{x} \in S_i} \mathbf{x} \) is the centroid of cluster \( S_i \).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: K-means seeks to minimize the total sum of squared dashed lines (inertia).

Lloyd's algorithm alternates two steps, each of which monotonically decreases \( J \):

  1. Assignment step: assign each point to the nearest centroid: \( S_i^{(t)} = \{\mathbf{x} : \|\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i^{(t)}\|_2 \le \|\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{(t)}\|_2,\; \forall j\} \)
  2. Update step: recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points

Convergence is guaranteed (finitely many partitions, \( J \) decreases monotonically), but only to a local minimum. Finding the global minimum is NP-hard.

Initialization matters. Random initialization can lead to poor local minima. K-Means++ (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007) selects initial centroids with probability proportional to squared distance from existing centroids, achieving an \( O(\log k) \)-competitive approximation to the optimal \( J \) in expectation.

Choosing \( k \). The elbow method plots \( J \) against \( k \) and looks for a diminishing-returns "elbow." The silhouette score measures how similar points are to their own cluster versus neighboring clusters. Neither is definitive; domain knowledge remains essential.

Connections. K-Means can be viewed as a special case of the EM algorithm applied to a Gaussian Mixture Model with equal, fixed, isotropic covariances. The WCSS objective is also related to the trace of the within-cluster scatter matrix \( \text{tr}(S_W) \) used in Fisher's Linear Discriminant Analysis.

Python 
import numpy as np

def kmeans(X, k, max_iter=100):
    # K-Means++ initialization
    centroids = [X[np.random.randint(len(X))]]
    for _ in range(1, k):
        dists = np.min([np.sum((X - c)**2, axis=1) for c in centroids], axis=0)
        probs = dists / dists.sum()
        centroids.append(X[np.random.choice(len(X), p=probs)])
    centroids = np.array(centroids)

    for _ in range(max_iter):
        # Assignment step
        dists = np.linalg.norm(X[:, None] - centroids[None], axis=2)
        labels = np.argmin(dists, axis=1)
        # Update step
        new_centroids = np.array([X[labels == i].mean(axis=0) for i in range(k)])
        if np.allclose(centroids, new_centroids):
            break
        centroids = new_centroids
    return labels, centroids

title: The Curse of Dimensionality
summary: A collection of phenomena arising in high-dimensional spaces where the exponential growth of volume makes data sparse, distances nearly uniform, and many intuitions from low-dimensional geometry break down. These effects severely degrade…

papers.citation: 1957 · Richard Bellman · Dynamic Programming

sections.body:
A collection of phenomena arising in high-dimensional spaces where the exponential growth of volume makes data sparse, distances nearly uniform, and many intuitions from low-dimensional geometry break down. These effects severely degrade distance-based algorithms such as k-nearest neighbours and k-means clustering.

The term was coined by Richard Bellman in the context of dynamic programming, where the state space grows exponentially with the number of state variables. In machine learning, the curse manifests in several concrete ways:

Volume concentration. The volume of a \(d\)-dimensional unit hypersphere relative to its bounding hypercube is:

\[ \frac{V_{\text{sphere}}}{V_{\text{cube}}} = \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{2^d \, \Gamma(d/2 + 1)} \]

This ratio approaches zero as \(d \to \infty\), meaning almost all volume concentrates in the "corners" of the hypercube, far from the centre. For example, in \(d = 10\) dimensions the hypersphere occupies only 0.25% of the hypercube's volume.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: As dimensions increase, the volume of the inscribed hypersphere shrinks rapidly to zero relative to its bounding hypercube. The corners dominate.

Distance concentration. For points drawn uniformly in a high-dimensional cube, the ratio of the maximum to minimum pairwise Euclidean distance converges to 1:

\[ \lim_{d \to \infty} \frac{D_{\max} - D_{\min}}{D_{\min}} \to 0 \]

When all distances are nearly equal, nearest-neighbour queries become meaningless because the "nearest" neighbour is barely closer than the farthest point. Beyer et al. (1999) formalised this, showing that for many data distributions Euclidean distance becomes uninformative in high dimensions.

Sample complexity. To maintain a fixed density of data points in \(d\) dimensions, the required sample size grows exponentially as \(O(k^d)\) where \(k\) is the number of points per axis. For 10 points per axis in 20 dimensions, one would need \(10^{20}\) samples.

Hughes phenomenon. In supervised classification, adding features initially improves performance, but beyond a critical point (determined by training set size), performance degrades because the model overfits to the increasingly sparse high-dimensional space. Hughes (1968) demonstrated this empirically for parametric classifiers.

Empty space is everywhere. A related intuition: to capture a fraction \(f\) of the data's volume in a \(d\)-dimensional unit cube with a subcube, the subcube's edge length must be \(f^{1/d}\). To cover just 1% of the volume in 100 dimensions requires an edge length of \(0.01^{1/100} \approx 0.955\) — nearly the entire range of each feature. Local methods must become effectively global in high dimensions.

Practical mitigations include dimensionality reduction (PCA, t-SNE, autoencoders), feature selection, and regularisation. Tree-based methods and neural networks are less affected than pure distance-based algorithms because they learn adaptive, data-dependent representations rather than relying on global distance metrics. The manifold hypothesis — that high-dimensional data often lies on or near a low-dimensional manifold — is the reason deep learning can succeed despite nominally high-dimensional inputs.

title: Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)
summary: A class of algorithms for sampling from complex probability distributions by constructing a Markov chain whose stationary distribution equals the desired target distribution. MCMC is indispensable in Bayesian inference when the posterior…

papers.citation: 1953 · Metropolis, Rosenbluth, Rosenbluth, Teller, Teller · Equation of State Calculations by Fast Computing Machines

sections.body:
A class of algorithms for sampling from complex probability distributions by constructing a Markov chain whose stationary distribution equals the desired target distribution. MCMC is indispensable in Bayesian inference when the posterior is analytically intractable.

In Bayesian inference we want to compute the posterior \( p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)\,p(\theta) \), but the normalising constant (evidence) \( p(\mathcal{D}) = \int p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)\,p(\theta)\,d\theta \) is often intractable in high dimensions. MCMC sidesteps this by generating correlated samples from the posterior without evaluating the evidence.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The Markov chain proposes a random jump. Moves towards higher probability are always accepted; moves towards lower probability are accepted with a probability ratio.

Metropolis-Hastings algorithm. The most widely known MCMC method. Given the current state \(x\), propose a candidate \(x'\) from a proposal distribution \(q(x' \mid x)\) and accept it with probability:

\[ A(x, x') = \min\!\left(1,\; \frac{p(x')\,q(x \mid x')}{p(x)\,q(x' \mid x)}\right) \]

where \(p(\cdot)\) is the target distribution (known up to a normalising constant). The ratio cancels the unknown constant, which is the key insight. If the proposal is symmetric (\(q(x' \mid x) = q(x \mid x')\)), this reduces to the original Metropolis algorithm.

Gibbs sampling is a special case of Metropolis-Hastings where each variable is sampled from its full conditional distribution, and the acceptance probability is always 1. It is efficient when conditionals are easy to sample but the joint is not.

Convergence diagnostics. Samples drawn before the chain reaches its stationary distribution (the burn-in period) are discarded. Common diagnostics include the Gelman-Rubin \(\hat{R}\) statistic (comparing within-chain and between-chain variance across multiple runs) and trace plots. Autocorrelation between successive samples reduces effective sample size, motivating thinning or more advanced samplers like Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC).

Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) augments the state space with momentum variables and simulates Hamiltonian dynamics to propose distant states with high acceptance probability, dramatically reducing random-walk behaviour in high dimensions.

title: Reparameterization Trick (VAE)
summary: A technique that enables backpropagation through stochastic sampling nodes by expressing the random variable as a deterministic, differentiable function of the distribution's parameters and an independent noise source. It is essential fo…

papers.citation: 2013 · Kingma, Welling · Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes

sections.body:
A technique that enables backpropagation through stochastic sampling nodes by expressing the random variable as a deterministic, differentiable function of the distribution's parameters and an independent noise source. It is essential for training Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) end-to-end with gradient descent.

In a VAE, the encoder outputs parameters \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) of an approximate posterior \(q_\phi(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 I)\). To decode, we need a sample \(z \sim q_\phi(z \mid x)\), but sampling is a non-differentiable operation — gradients cannot flow through a stochastic node back to \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Deterministic transformation of an external noise source \(\varepsilon\) allows backpropagation through \(z\) back to \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\).

The trick. Instead of sampling \(z\) directly, we sample noise from a fixed distribution and transform it deterministically:

\[ \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I), \qquad z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon \]

where \(\odot\) denotes element-wise multiplication. Now \(z\) is a deterministic function of \(\mu\), \(\sigma\), and \(\varepsilon\). Because \(\varepsilon\) does not depend on the model parameters \(\phi\), the gradients \(\partial z / \partial \mu = I\) and \(\partial z / \partial \sigma = \varepsilon\) are well-defined, and standard backpropagation applies.

Why it matters. Without reparameterization, one must resort to high-variance score-function estimators (REINFORCE) for the gradient of the ELBO. The reparameterization trick yields a low-variance, unbiased gradient estimator that makes VAE training practical.

Generalisation. The trick applies to any location-scale family (e.g., Gaussian, Laplace). For distributions without a simple reparameterization (e.g., discrete distributions), alternatives like the Gumbel-Softmax trick or straight-through estimators are used.

The full VAE objective (ELBO) that this trick enables optimising is:

\[ \mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; x) = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z|x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] - D_{\text{KL}}(q_\phi(z \mid x) \| p(z)) \]

where the first term is the reconstruction loss and the second is the KL divergence regulariser pushing the posterior toward the prior \(p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)\).

Derivations

The gradient of the ELBO with respect to \(\phi\) requires differentiating through the expectation over \(q_\phi(z \mid x)\). Using the reparameterization \(z = g(\varepsilon, \phi) = \mu_\phi + \sigma_\phi \odot \varepsilon\):

\[ \nabla_\phi \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z|x)}[f(z)] = \nabla_\phi \mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)}[f(g(\varepsilon, \phi))] = \mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon}[\nabla_\phi f(g(\varepsilon, \phi))] \]

The expectation is now over a distribution that does not depend on \(\phi\), so the gradient passes inside and can be estimated by Monte Carlo with a single sample of \(\varepsilon\).

title: Autoencoders
summary: Neural networks trained to reconstruct their input through a lower-dimensional bottleneck, forcing the model to learn a compressed latent representation that captures the most salient features of the data distribution. They serve as a no…

papers.citation: 1986 · Rumelhart, Hinton, Williams · Learning Internal Representations by Error Propagation

sections.body:
Neural networks trained to reconstruct their input through a lower-dimensional bottleneck, forcing the model to learn a compressed latent representation that captures the most salient features of the data distribution. They serve as a nonlinear generalisation of PCA.

An autoencoder consists of two components:

  • Encoder \(f_\theta\): maps input \(x \in \mathbb{R}^n\) to a latent code \(h = f_\theta(x) \in \mathbb{R}^d\) where \(d < n\).
  • Decoder \(g_\phi\): reconstructs the input as \(\hat{x} = g_\phi(h) \in \mathbb{R}^n\).

The standard training objective minimises the reconstruction loss:

\[ \mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x}) = \|x - g_\phi(f_\theta(x))\|^2 \]

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The hourglass architecture forces information through a narrow bottleneck \(d < n\), learning an efficient compressed representation.

If both encoder and decoder are linear (single layer, no activation), the optimal solution recovers the subspace spanned by the top \(d\) principal components of the data — a result proven by Baldi and Hornik (1989). With nonlinear activations, autoencoders learn richer, curved manifolds that can capture structure PCA misses entirely.

sections.title: Variants and extensions
sections.body:
Variant Key idea Regularisation term
Denoising AE Input is corrupted with noise; model learns to reconstruct the clean version, improving robustness Implicit (via corrupted input \(\tilde{x}\))
Sparse AE Adds a sparsity penalty to the bottleneck, enabling overcomplete representations (\(d > n\)) \(\lambda \sum_j \text{KL}(\rho \| \hat{\rho}_j)\) on average activations
Variational AE (VAE) Encoder outputs distribution parameters; latent space is regularised, enabling generation \(D_{\text{KL}}(q_\phi(z|x) \| p(z))\)
Contractive AE Penalises sensitivity of the encoder to input perturbations, encouraging locally invariant features \(\lambda \| J_f(x) \|_F^2\) (Frobenius norm of encoder Jacobian)
VQ-VAE Discrete latent codes via vector quantisation; avoids posterior collapse and enables high-fidelity generation Codebook commitment loss

Connection to the manifold hypothesis. Autoencoders implicitly assume that the data lies on or near a low-dimensional manifold embedded in \(\mathbb{R}^n\). The encoder learns a coordinate system on this manifold, and the decoder learns the embedding map back to the ambient space. This perspective explains why autoencoders succeed on images and speech despite the nominally high dimensionality of those inputs.

Applications include dimensionality reduction, anomaly detection (high reconstruction error signals out-of-distribution inputs), denoising, and pretraining representations for downstream tasks. Autoencoders are also the backbone of VAEs and VQ-VAEs used in modern generative modelling, and the encoder-decoder structure itself has influenced the design of sequence-to-sequence models and the Transformer architecture.

title: GAN Minimax Objective
summary: The game-theoretic training objective for Generative Adversarial Networks, in which a generator \(G\) and discriminator \(D\) compete in a two-player minimax game. At the Nash equilibrium, \(G\) produces samples indistinguishable from re…

papers.citation: 2014 · Goodfellow, Pouget-Abadie, Mirza, Xu, Warde-Farley, Ozair, Courville, Bengio · Generative Adversarial Nets

sections.body:
The game-theoretic training objective for Generative Adversarial Networks, in which a generator \(G\) and discriminator \(D\) compete in a two-player minimax game. At the Nash equilibrium, \(G\) produces samples indistinguishable from real data and \(D\) outputs \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) everywhere.

The GAN framework casts generative modelling as a zero-sum game between two networks:

  • Generator \(G(z;\, \theta_g)\): maps noise \(z \sim p_z(z)\) (typically standard Gaussian) to synthetic data samples.
  • Discriminator \(D(x;\, \theta_d)\): outputs the probability that input \(x\) came from the real data distribution rather than from \(G\).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The discriminator tries to distinguish Real from Fake. The generator backpropagates through the discriminator to update its weights to make generated samples more "real".

The minimax value function is:

\[ \min_G \max_D \; V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))] \]

Optimal discriminator. For a fixed \(G\), the optimal discriminator is:

\[ D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} \]

Substituting \(D^*\) into the value function shows that the generator minimises the Jensen-Shannon divergence between the real and generated distributions:

\[ V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot D_{\text{JS}}(p_{\text{data}} \| p_g) \]

The global minimum is achieved when \(p_g = p_{\text{data}}\), at which point \(D_{\text{JS}} = 0\) and \(V = -\log 4\).

Training instability. In practice, minimising \(\log(1 - D(G(z)))\) saturates when \(G\) is weak (gradients vanish). A common heuristic replaces the generator loss with \(-\log D(G(z))\) (the "non-saturating" variant), which provides stronger gradients early in training. Mode collapse — where \(G\) maps many noise inputs to a few outputs — remains a persistent challenge, motivating extensions like Wasserstein GAN (which replaces JS divergence with the Earth Mover's distance) and spectral normalisation.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Optimal discriminator derivation. For fixed \(G\), the objective for \(D\) is:

\[ \max_D \int_x \left[ p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \right] dx \]

Taking the derivative with respect to \(D(x)\) and setting it to zero:

\[ \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} = 0 \implies D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} \]

title: Q-Learning
summary: A model-free, off-policy reinforcement learning algorithm that learns the optimal action-value function \(Q^*(s, a)\) directly, without requiring a model of the environment's dynamics. It is the foundation of modern deep RL methods such…

papers.citation: 1989 · Chris Watkins · Learning from Delayed Rewards (PhD thesis)

sections.body:
A model-free, off-policy reinforcement learning algorithm that learns the optimal action-value function \(Q^*(s, a)\) directly, without requiring a model of the environment's dynamics. It is the foundation of modern deep RL methods such as DQN.

Q-learning maintains a table (or function approximator) of action-value estimates \(Q(s, a)\) representing the expected cumulative discounted reward of taking action \(a\) in state \(s\) and thereafter following the optimal policy. The key update rule is a temporal-difference (TD) update:

\[ Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t, A_t) \right] \]

where \(\alpha \in (0, 1]\) is the learning rate, \(\gamma \in [0, 1]\) is the discount factor, \(R_{t+1}\) is the immediate reward, and the term in brackets is the TD error.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Because it uses \(\max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')\), the algorithm learns about the optimal policy regardless of the exploratory behavior policy actually generating the transitions.

Off-policy nature. The update uses \(\max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')\) — the value of the greedy action in the next state — regardless of which action the agent actually takes. This means the agent can follow an exploratory behaviour policy (e.g., \(\varepsilon\)-greedy) while learning the optimal policy. This contrasts with SARSA, an on-policy algorithm that updates toward the value of the action actually chosen: \(Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})\).

Convergence. Tabular Q-learning converges to \(Q^*\) with probability 1 under the Robbins-Monro conditions on the learning rate (\(\sum \alpha_t = \infty\), \(\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty\)) and provided every state-action pair is visited infinitely often.

Deep Q-Networks (DQN). Mnih et al. (2015) replaced the Q-table with a deep neural network, introducing experience replay (breaking correlations between consecutive samples) and a target network (stabilising the bootstrap target) to make Q-learning work at scale. DQN achieved human-level performance on many Atari games.

Property Q-Learning SARSA
Policy type Off-policy On-policy
Update target \(\max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')\) \(Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})\)
Behaviour near cliffs Learns optimal (risky) path Learns safer path under exploration

title: The Bellman Equation
summary: A recursive equation that decomposes the value of a state (or state-action pair) into the immediate reward plus the discounted value of successor states. It is the mathematical backbone of nearly all reinforcement learning and dynamic pr…

papers.citation: 1957 · Richard Bellman · Dynamic Programming

sections.body:
A recursive equation that decomposes the value of a state (or state-action pair) into the immediate reward plus the discounted value of successor states. It is the mathematical backbone of nearly all reinforcement learning and dynamic programming algorithms.

Bellman expectation equation for \(V^\pi\). For a policy \(\pi\), the state-value function satisfies:

\[ V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V^\pi(s') \right] \]

where \(\gamma \in [0, 1]\) is the discount factor, \(p(s', r \mid s, a)\) is the environment's transition-reward dynamics, and \(\pi(a \mid s)\) is the probability of action \(a\) in state \(s\) under policy \(\pi\).

Bellman expectation equation for \(Q^\pi\). The action-value form is:

\[ Q^\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q^\pi(s', a') \right] \]

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The value of the top node \(s\) is the expectation over actions (via the policy) and subsequent transitions, collecting immediate rewards and discounted values of the leaf nodes \(s'\).

Bellman optimality equations. The optimal value functions \(V^*\) and \(Q^*\) satisfy:

\[ V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V^*(s') \right] \] \[ Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \right] \]

The optimality equations replace the policy-weighted average over actions with a \(\max\), reflecting the fact that the optimal policy always selects the best action.

Connection to algorithms. Value iteration solves the Bellman optimality equation by iterative application. Policy iteration alternates between evaluating \(V^\pi\) (solving the expectation equation) and improving the policy greedily. Q-learning approximates the optimality equation for \(Q^*\) via sample-based temporal-difference updates. The Bellman equation is also the basis for the loss function in DQN, where the TD error is the Bellman residual.

title: The Markov Property
summary: The memoryless property stating that the conditional distribution of future states depends only on the present state and action, not on the history of preceding states. It is the foundational assumption underpinning Markov Decision Proce…

papers.citation: 1906 · Andrey Markov · Extension of the Law of Large Numbers to Dependent Quantities

sections.body:
The memoryless property stating that the conditional distribution of future states depends only on the present state and action, not on the history of preceding states. It is the foundational assumption underpinning Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) and most reinforcement learning theory.

Formally, a state \(S_t\) satisfies the Markov property if and only if:

\[ P(S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t, S_{t-1}, A_{t-1}, \ldots, S_0, A_0) = P(S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t) \]

In words: the current state is a sufficient statistic for the future. All information needed to predict the next state is encoded in the present state; the entire trajectory history is redundant.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Because \(S_t\) encapsulates all historical information relevant to the future evolution of the system, the past is conditionally independent from the future given the present.

Markov Decision Processes. When an environment satisfies the Markov property, it can be modelled as an MDP, defined by the tuple \((\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, p, r, \gamma)\):

  • \(\mathcal{S}\): state space
  • \(\mathcal{A}\): action space
  • \(p(s' \mid s, a)\): transition dynamics
  • \(r(s, a)\): reward function
  • \(\gamma\): discount factor

The Markov assumption enables the recursive decomposition in the Bellman equation, which in turn underpins dynamic programming, policy iteration, value iteration, and temporal-difference methods like Q-learning and SARSA.

When the property fails. Many real-world environments are not truly Markov (e.g., a game where past moves matter but are not encoded in the current observation). Common strategies include:

  • State augmentation: stacking past observations to form an approximately Markov state (as in DQN's frame-stacking for Atari).
  • Recurrent architectures: using LSTMs or GRUs to maintain a hidden state that summarises history.
  • Partially Observable MDPs (POMDPs): the formal framework for non-Markov observations, which maintains a belief state over the true underlying state.

The Markov property also underlies Markov chains and is central to MCMC methods, Hidden Markov Models, and the theory of stochastic processes more broadly.

title: Bootstrap Aggregating (Bagging)
summary: An ensemble meta-algorithm that reduces variance and mitigates overfitting by training multiple models on bootstrap samples of the training data and aggregating their predictions. Random Forest is its most prominent instantiation.

papers.citation: 1996 · Leo Breiman · Bagging Predictors

sections.body:
An ensemble meta-algorithm that reduces variance and mitigates overfitting by training multiple models on bootstrap samples of the training data and aggregating their predictions. Random Forest is its most prominent instantiation.

Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) exploits a key insight from statistics: averaging over multiple noisy but approximately unbiased estimators reduces variance without increasing bias. Given a training set \(\mathcal{D}\) of size \(n\), the algorithm proceeds as follows:

  1. Draw \(B\) bootstrap samples \(\mathcal{D}^{*1}, \ldots, \mathcal{D}^{*B}\), each formed by sampling \(n\) points from \(\mathcal{D}\) uniformly with replacement.
  2. Train a base learner \(\hat{f}^{*b}\) on each bootstrap sample independently.
  3. Aggregate predictions at test time.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Models are trained independently in parallel on resampled datasets, decorrelating their errors.

For regression, the bagged prediction is the average:

\[ \hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}^{*b}(x) \]

For classification, the prediction is the majority vote (or the averaged class probabilities).

Variance reduction. If the base learners have variance \(\sigma^2\) and pairwise correlation \(\rho\), the variance of the bagged ensemble is:

\[ \text{Var}\!\left[\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right] = \rho \sigma^2 + \frac{1 - \rho}{B} \sigma^2 \]

As \(B \to \infty\), the second term vanishes, leaving \(\rho \sigma^2\). This reveals the fundamental limitation: bagging cannot reduce variance below the correlated component. Random Forests address this by introducing random feature subsampling at each split, thereby reducing \(\rho\).

Out-of-bag (OOB) estimation. Each bootstrap sample omits roughly \(1 - 1/e \approx 36.8\%\) of the original data. These "out-of-bag" samples provide a free, nearly unbiased estimate of generalisation error without a separate validation set — each training point is predicted only by the trees that did not include it.

When bagging helps. Bagging is most effective with high-variance, low-bias base learners (e.g., deep, unpruned decision trees). It has little effect on stable learners like linear regression or k-nearest neighbours, because their low variance leaves nothing to reduce.

Property Bagging Boosting
Training Parallel (independent models) Sequential (each corrects predecessor)
Error reduction Reduces variance Reduces bias and variance
Base learners Strong (high variance) Weak (high bias, e.g., stumps)
Overfitting risk Low — more trees never hurt Higher — can overfit with too many rounds

title: Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM)
summary: An ensemble method that builds a strong predictive model by sequentially fitting weak learners (typically shallow decision trees) to the negative gradient of a differentiable loss function. Each new tree corrects the residual errors of t…

papers.citation: 1999 · Jerome Friedman · Greedy Function Approximation: A Gradient Boosting Machine

sections.body:
An ensemble method that builds a strong predictive model by sequentially fitting weak learners (typically shallow decision trees) to the negative gradient of a differentiable loss function. Each new tree corrects the residual errors of the current ensemble, performing gradient descent in function space.

Unlike bagging, which trains models independently and reduces variance, gradient boosting builds models additively and primarily reduces bias. The framework is remarkably general: any differentiable loss function can be used, making GBM applicable to regression, classification, ranking, and survival analysis.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Each weak learner \(h_m\) targets the remaining error (pseudo-residuals) of the cumulative ensemble \(F_{m-1}\).

Algorithm. Starting from an initial constant model \(F_0(x) = \arg\min_c \sum_{i=1}^n L(y_i, c)\), for each stage \(m = 1, \ldots, M\):

  1. Compute pseudo-residuals (negative gradients): \[ r_{im} = -\left[\frac{\partial L(y_i, F(x_i))}{\partial F(x_i)}\right]_{F = F_{m-1}} \quad \text{for } i = 1, \ldots, n \]
  2. Fit a weak learner \(h_m(x)\) to the pseudo-residuals \(\{(x_i, r_{im})\}\).
  3. Compute the optimal step size: \(\gamma_m = \arg\min_\gamma \sum_{i=1}^n L(y_i,\, F_{m-1}(x_i) + \gamma\, h_m(x_i))\)
  4. Update the model: \(F_m(x) = F_{m-1}(x) + \nu \cdot \gamma_m\, h_m(x)\)

where \(\nu \in (0, 1]\) is the learning rate (shrinkage parameter). Smaller values of \(\nu\) require more trees but generally improve generalisation via regularisation.

Why "gradient" boosting? The pseudo-residuals \(r_{im}\) are the negative gradient of the loss with respect to the model's prediction. Fitting a tree to these residuals and adding it to the ensemble is equivalent to taking a gradient descent step in function space. For squared loss \(L = \frac{1}{2}(y - F(x))^2\), the pseudo-residuals simplify to the ordinary residuals \(y_i - F_{m-1}(x_i)\).

sections.title: Key hyperparameters and regularisation
sections.body:
Hyperparameter Effect
Number of trees \(M\) More trees reduce training error; too many overfit (use early stopping)
Learning rate \(\nu\) Smaller values regularise but require more trees
Tree depth Controls interaction order; depth \(d\) captures \(d\)-way interactions
Subsampling fraction Stochastic GBM: training each tree on a random subset reduces variance
Min samples per leaf Prevents overly specific splits

Modern implementations. XGBoost (2016) introduced second-order Taylor expansion of the loss, regularised objective, and systems-level optimisations (column blocking, cache-aware access). LightGBM uses histogram-based splitting and leaf-wise tree growth for speed. CatBoost handles categorical features natively via ordered target statistics. These implementations dominate tabular data benchmarks and competitive ML.

Python 
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier

gbm = GradientBoostingClassifier(
    n_estimators=200,
    learning_rate=0.1,
    max_depth=3,
    subsample=0.8,
    random_state=42,
)
gbm.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(f"Test accuracy: {gbm.score(X_test, y_test):.3f}")

title: The Fisher Information Matrix
summary: A matrix from mathematical statistics that quantifies how much information an observable random variable carries about unknown parameters of its distribution. In machine learning, it governs the geometry of parameter space and underpins…

papers.citation: 1925 · Ronald A. Fisher · Theory of Statistical Estimation 1998 · Shun-Ichi Amari · Natural Gradient Works Efficiently in Learning

sections.body:
A matrix from mathematical statistics that quantifies how much information an observable random variable carries about unknown parameters of its distribution. In machine learning, it governs the geometry of parameter space and underpins Natural Gradient Descent.

Given a parametric model \( p(x|\theta) \), the score function is the gradient of the log-likelihood with respect to parameters: \( s(\theta) = \nabla_\theta \log p(x|\theta) \). The score has zero mean under the model, i.e. \( \mathbb{E}_{p(x|\theta)}[s(\theta)] = 0 \). The Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) is defined as the covariance of the score:

\[ \mathbf{F}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{p(x|\theta)}\!\left[\nabla_\theta \log p(x|\theta)\;\nabla_\theta \log p(x|\theta)^T\right] \]

where \( \mathbf{F}(\theta) \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d} \) for \( d \) parameters. Under regularity conditions (interchange of differentiation and integration), an equivalent form relates the FIM to the curvature of the log-likelihood:

\[ \mathbf{F}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{p(x|\theta)}\!\left[\nabla_\theta^2 \log p(x|\theta)\right] \]

This shows the FIM equals the expected negative Hessian of the log-likelihood — it measures how sharply the log-likelihood curves around \( \theta \), and thus how "informative" the data is about the parameters.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: A highly curved log-likelihood (left) corresponds to a large Fisher Information Matrix, indicating \(\theta\) can be estimated with high precision.

sections.title: Key properties and applications in ML
sections.body:
• Cramér–Rao bound: For any unbiased estimator \( \hat\theta \), the variance is lower-bounded by the inverse FIM: \( \text{Var}(\hat\theta) \geq \mathbf{F}(\theta)^{-1} \). This sets the fundamental limit on estimation precision.
  • Natural Gradient Descent: Ordinary gradient descent treats parameter space as Euclidean, but the FIM defines a Riemannian metric on the statistical manifold of distributions. Natural gradient descent preconditions the gradient by the inverse FIM: \( \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\,\mathbf{F}(\theta_t)^{-1}\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L} \). This makes updates invariant to reparameterization and typically converges faster in regions of high curvature. Amari (1998) formalized this connection.
  • Relation to KL divergence: The FIM is the Hessian of the KL divergence between nearby distributions: \( D_{\text{KL}}(p_\theta \| p_{\theta+\delta}) \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\delta^T \mathbf{F}(\theta)\,\delta \). This makes it the local metric for measuring distance between probability distributions.
  • Practical approximations: Computing and inverting the full FIM is \( O(d^2) \) in memory and \( O(d^3) \) to invert, which is infeasible for modern neural networks with millions of parameters. Approximations include diagonal FIM, block-diagonal (K-FAC by Martens & Grosse, 2015), and the empirical Fisher (using data samples rather than model expectations). These are used in second-order optimizers, Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) for continual learning, and Laplace approximation for Bayesian deep learning.

The FIM connects to the Hessian of the loss in supervised learning: when the loss is the negative log-likelihood, the Hessian decomposes into a term involving the FIM (the Gauss–Newton component) plus residual terms. This is why the FIM is sometimes used as a curvature proxy in optimization.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Equivalence of the two FIM forms. Starting from the score identity \( \mathbb{E}[ abla_ heta \log p(x| heta)] = 0 \), differentiate both sides with respect to \( heta \):

\[ 0 = abla_ heta \int p(x| heta)\, abla_ heta \log p(x| heta)\,dx = \int p(x| heta)\left[ abla_ heta^2 \log p(x| heta) + abla_ heta \log p(x| heta)\, abla_ heta \log p(x| heta)^T ight]dx \]

Therefore:

\[ \mathbb{E}\!\left[ abla_ heta \log p\; abla_ heta \log p^T ight] = -\mathbb{E}\!\left[ abla_ heta^2 \log p ight] \]

which shows the outer-product form equals the negative expected Hessian form.

title: AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting)
summary: An ensemble method that sequentially trains weak classifiers (typically decision stumps), reweighting training examples after each round so that misclassified instances receive more attention. The final prediction is a weighted majority…

papers.citation: 1997 · Yoav Freund, Robert Schapire · A Decision-Theoretic Generalization of On-Line Learning and an Application to Boosting 2000 · Jerome Friedman, Trevor Hastie, Robert Tibshirani · Additive Logistic Regression: a Statistical View of Boosting

sections.body:
An ensemble method that sequentially trains weak classifiers (typically decision stumps), reweighting training examples after each round so that misclassified instances receive more attention. The final prediction is a weighted majority vote of all weak learners.

AdaBoost was introduced by Freund and Schapire (1997) and was one of the first practical boosting algorithms, proving that combining many "slightly better than random" classifiers can yield arbitrarily high accuracy. It won the Gödel Prize in 2003 for its theoretical significance.

Algorithm. Given training set \( \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^N \) with \( y_i \in \{-1, +1\} \):

  1. Initialize sample weights uniformly: \( w_i^{(1)} = \frac{1}{N} \).
  2. For each round \( t = 1, \ldots, T \):
    • Train weak learner \( h_t(x) \) on the weighted dataset.
    • Compute weighted classification error: \( \varepsilon_t = \sum_{i=1}^N w_i^{(t)}\,\mathbb{1}[h_t(x_i) \neq y_i] \).
    • Compute classifier weight: \( \alpha_t = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}\right) \). Note: \( \alpha_t > 0 \) when \( \varepsilon_t < 0.5 \) (better than random), and \( \alpha_t \to \infty \) as \( \varepsilon_t \to 0 \).
    • Update sample weights: \( w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \exp\!\bigl(-\alpha_t\, y_i\, h_t(x_i)\bigr) \), then renormalize so weights sum to 1. Misclassified examples (where \( y_i h_t(x_i) = -1 \)) have their weights multiplied by \( e^{\alpha_t} \); correct ones by \( e^{-\alpha_t} \).
  3. Final classifier: \( H(x) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^T \alpha_t\, h_t(x)\right) \).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: In each round, the algorithm increases the weight of misclassified examples (larger symbols), forcing subsequent weak learners to focus on the hardest cases.

sections.title: Theoretical properties
sections.body:
• Training error bound: The training error of the ensemble decreases exponentially with rounds: \( \text{err}_{\text{train}} \leq \exp\!\left(-2\sum_{t=1}^T \gamma_t^2\right) \) where \( \gamma_t = \frac{1}{2} - \varepsilon_t \) is the "edge" of each weak learner over random guessing.
  • Exponential loss minimization: Breiman (and later Friedman, Hastie, Tibshirani 2000) showed that AdaBoost is equivalent to forward stagewise additive modeling of the exponential loss \( \mathcal{L} = \sum_i \exp(-y_i F(x_i)) \) where \( F(x) = \sum_t \alpha_t h_t(x) \). This connection to loss minimization opened the door to Gradient Boosting.
  • Margin theory: Schapire et al. (1998) showed AdaBoost tends to maximize the margin of training examples, which partially explains its resistance to overfitting in practice even as the number of rounds grows — though it can overfit with noisy data.

Connections: AdaBoost is a special case of the more general Gradient Boosting framework (Friedman, 2001) using exponential loss. Unlike Random Forests (which reduce variance via bagging), boosting methods like AdaBoost reduce bias by iteratively correcting errors. XGBoost and LightGBM are modern descendants that use different loss functions and tree-building strategies.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Deriving \( \alpha_t \) from exponential loss. At round \( t \), we want to find \( \alpha_t \) that minimizes:

\[ \sum_{i=1}^N w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha\, y_i\, h_t(x_i)) \]

Split the sum into correct (\( y_i h_t = +1 \)) and incorrect (\( y_i h_t = -1 \)) examples:

\[ = e^{-\alpha}(1 - \varepsilon_t) + e^{\alpha}\,\varepsilon_t \]

Taking the derivative with respect to \( \alpha \) and setting to zero:

\[ -e^{-\alpha}(1-\varepsilon_t) + e^{\alpha}\varepsilon_t = 0 \implies e^{2\alpha} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t} \implies \alpha_t = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}\right) \]

sections.title: Python
sections.body:
import numpy as np

def adaboost(X, y, T, weak_learner_cls):
    '''AdaBoost for binary classification (y in {-1, +1}).'''
    N = len(y)
    w = np.ones(N) / N
    alphas, learners = [], []

    for t in range(T):
        h = weak_learner_cls()
        h.fit(X, y, sample_weight=w)
        preds = h.predict(X)
        err = np.dot(w, preds != y)
        err = np.clip(err, 1e-10, 1 - 1e-10)
        alpha = 0.5 * np.log((1 - err) / err)
        w *= np.exp(-alpha * y * preds)
        w /= w.sum()
        alphas.append(alpha)
        learners.append(h)

    def predict(X_new):
        return np.sign(sum(a * h.predict(X_new)
                           for a, h in zip(alphas, learners)))
    return predict

title: Information Gain (Decision Trees)
summary: The expected reduction in entropy achieved by partitioning a dataset on a given attribute. It is the splitting criterion used by the ID3 and C4.5 decision tree algorithms to greedily select the most informative feature at each node.

papers.citation: 1986 · J. Ross Quinlan · Induction of Decision Trees (Machine Learning, Vol. 1) 1984 · Leo Breiman et al. · Classification and Regression Trees

sections.body:
The expected reduction in entropy achieved by partitioning a dataset on a given attribute. It is the splitting criterion used by the ID3 and C4.5 decision tree algorithms to greedily select the most informative feature at each node.

Entropy measures the impurity (uncertainty) of a set \( S \) with \( c \) classes:

\[ H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i \]

where \( p_i \) is the proportion of class \( i \) in \( S \). Entropy is 0 for a pure node (all one class) and maximal at \( \log_2 c \) for uniform class distribution.

Information Gain for attribute \( A \) with possible values \( \text{Values}(A) \) is:

\[ IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{|S_v|}{|S|}\,H(S_v) \]

where \( S_v \subseteq S \) is the subset of examples where attribute \( A \) takes value \( v \). The algorithm greedily selects the attribute \( A^* = \arg\max_A IG(S, A) \) at each node.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Splitting on Feature A separates the classes well, dropping the average entropy. The difference is the Information Gain.

Concrete example. Consider a binary classification dataset with 14 examples: 9 positive, 5 negative. Root entropy is \( H = -\frac{9}{14}\log_2\frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14}\log_2\frac{5}{14} \approx 0.940 \) bits. If splitting on attribute "Outlook" (Sunny/Overcast/Rain) produces subsets with entropies 0.971, 0.0, 0.971 and sizes 5, 4, 5 respectively, the information gain is \( 0.940 - \frac{5}{14}(0.971) - \frac{4}{14}(0) - \frac{5}{14}(0.971) \approx 0.247 \) bits.

sections.title: Limitations and alternatives
sections.body:
• Bias toward high-cardinality features: Information Gain favors attributes with many distinct values (e.g., an ID column achieves maximal IG but is useless for generalization). C4.5 (Quinlan, 1993) addresses this with the Gain Ratio, which normalizes IG by the intrinsic information (split information) of the attribute: \( GR(S, A) = \frac{IG(S, A)}{-\sum_v \frac{|S_v|}{|S|}\log_2 \frac{|S_v|}{|S|}} \).
  • Gini impurity: CART (Breiman et al., 1984) uses Gini impurity \( G(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^c p_i^2 \) instead of entropy. Gini is computationally cheaper (no logarithm) and in practice produces very similar trees. The Gini-based splitting criterion measures the reduction in Gini impurity rather than entropy.
  • Connection to mutual information: Information Gain is equivalent to the mutual information \( I(Y; A) \) between the class variable \( Y \) and attribute \( A \). This connects decision tree splitting to information-theoretic feature selection.

Information Gain is foundational to all tree-based ensemble methods (Random Forests, Gradient Boosted Trees) since individual trees in these ensembles use entropy or Gini-based splits internally.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Information Gain as mutual information. By definition of mutual information:

\[ I(Y; A) = H(Y) - H(Y|A) = H(Y) - \sum_{v} P(A=v)\,H(Y|A=v) \]

Since \( P(A=v) = \frac{|S_v|}{|S|} \) and \( H(Y|A=v) = H(S_v) \), this is exactly \( IG(S, A) \).

title: Dropout
summary: A regularization technique that randomly sets neuron activations to zero during training with probability \( 1 - p \), preventing co-adaptation of features and acting as an implicit ensemble over exponentially many thinned sub-networks.

papers.citation: 2014 · Srivastava, Hinton, Krizhevsky, Sutskever, Salakhutdinov · Dropout: A Simple Way to Prevent Neural Networks from Overfitting 2016 · Yarin Gal, Zoubin Ghahramani · Dropout as a Bayesian Approximation

sections.body:
A regularization technique that randomly sets neuron activations to zero during training with probability \( 1 - p \), preventing co-adaptation of features and acting as an implicit ensemble over exponentially many thinned sub-networks.

Dropout was introduced by Srivastava et al. (2014), building on earlier ideas by Hinton et al. (2012). The core motivation is that neural networks with many parameters tend to overfit by developing complex co-adaptations where specific neurons become dependent on the presence of other specific neurons. By randomly "dropping" neurons, each neuron must learn features that are independently useful.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Units (and their connections) are randomly suppressed during training, forcing the network to distribute learned representations.

Mechanism. During training, for each forward pass, each hidden unit \( h_j \) is multiplied by a Bernoulli random variable \( r_j \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p) \):

\[ \tilde{h}_j = r_j \cdot h_j \]

where \( p \) is the keep probability (typically 0.5 for hidden layers, 0.8 for input layers). The resulting thinned network is trained via backpropagation as usual. At test time, all units are active but outputs are scaled by \( p \) to match the expected training-time activation. Equivalently, inverted dropout (the standard modern implementation) scales activations by \( 1/p \) during training and requires no change at test time.

sections.title: Theoretical interpretations
sections.body:
• Ensemble averaging: A network with \( n \) units admits \( 2^n \) possible thinned sub-networks. Dropout approximately trains all of them with shared weights and averages their predictions at test time. This is analogous to bagging but with extreme weight sharing.
  • Bayesian approximation: Gal and Ghahramani (2016) showed that dropout training is mathematically equivalent to approximate variational inference in a deep Gaussian process. Applying dropout at test time (MC Dropout) yields calibrated uncertainty estimates.
  • Adaptive regularization: Wager et al. (2013) showed dropout on logistic regression is equivalent to an adaptive L2 penalty that is stronger for rare features, connecting it to classical regularization.

sections.title: Practical considerations
sections.body:
Layer type Typical dropout rate \( 1-p \) Notes
Input layer 0.1–0.2 Light dropout to preserve input signal
Hidden FC layers 0.5 The original default from Hinton et al.
Conv layers 0.1–0.3 Lower rates since convolutions share parameters; Spatial Dropout drops entire feature maps
Recurrent layers 0.2–0.5 Applied to non-recurrent connections; Variational Dropout (Gal & Ghahramani, 2016) uses same mask across time steps

Connections: In Transformers, dropout is typically applied to attention weights and after feed-forward sub-layers. DropConnect (Wan et al., 2013) generalizes dropout to dropping individual weights rather than activations. DropBlock (Ghiasi et al., 2018) drops contiguous regions in feature maps for CNNs. Batch Normalization has a similar regularizing effect and is sometimes used as a partial substitute.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Expected value at test time. During training, the expected activation of unit \( j \) is:

\[ \mathbb{E}[\tilde{h}_j] = \mathbb{E}[r_j] \cdot h_j = p \cdot h_j \]

At test time without dropout, the activation is \( h_j \). To match the training-time expected value, we scale by \( p \): \( \hat{h}_j = p \cdot h_j \). Inverted dropout instead divides by \( p \) during training: \( \tilde{h}_j = \frac{r_j}{p} \cdot h_j \), so \( \mathbb{E}[\tilde{h}_j] = h_j \), requiring no test-time adjustment.

title: Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)
summary: A matrix factorization that decomposes any \( m \times n \) real or complex matrix into the product of three matrices \( A = U\Sigma V^T \), generalizing eigendecomposition to non-square matrices. It is the mathematical foundation for PC…

papers.citation: 1873 · Beltrami, Jordan · Sulle funzioni bilineari (Giornale di Matematiche) 1965 · Gene Golub, William Kahan · Calculating the Singular Values and Pseudo-Inverse of a Matrix

sections.body:
A matrix factorization that decomposes any \( m \times n \) real or complex matrix into the product of three matrices \( A = U\Sigma V^T \), generalizing eigendecomposition to non-square matrices. It is the mathematical foundation for PCA, latent semantic analysis, low-rank approximation, and pseudoinverse computation.

SVD is one of the most important and universally applicable decompositions in linear algebra. It was developed over the 19th century by Beltrami (1873) and Jordan (1874), with computational algorithms refined by Golub and Kahan (1965) and Golub and Reinsch (1970).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: A data matrix \(A\) separates into orthonormal columns \(U\) (left singular vectors), diagonal \(\Sigma\) (singular values), and orthonormal rows \(V^T\) (right singular vectors).

Decomposition. Any matrix \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n} \) can be factored as:

\[ A = U \Sigma V^T \]

where:

  • \( U \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m} \) — orthogonal matrix whose columns are the left singular vectors (eigenvectors of \( AA^T \)).
  • \( \Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n} \) — diagonal matrix with non-negative entries \( \sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \cdots \geq \sigma_{\min(m,n)} \geq 0 \), the singular values (square roots of eigenvalues of \( A^TA \) or equivalently \( AA^T \)).
  • \( V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} \) — orthogonal matrix whose columns are the right singular vectors (eigenvectors of \( A^TA \)).

Geometric interpretation. Any linear transformation can be decomposed into three steps: (1) rotate/reflect the input space (\( V^T \)), (2) scale along coordinate axes (\( \Sigma \)), (3) rotate/reflect the output space (\( U \)). The singular values quantify how much the transformation stretches along each principal direction.

Truncated SVD and the Eckart–Young theorem. The rank-\( k \) truncated SVD \( A_k = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^T \) (keeping only the top \( k \) singular values) provides the optimal rank-\( k \) approximation of \( A \) in both Frobenius and spectral norms:

\[ A_k = \arg\min_{\text{rank}(B) = k} \|A - B\|_F \quad \text{with} \quad \|A - A_k\|_F = \sqrt{\sigma_{k+1}^2 + \cdots + \sigma_r^2} \]

where \( r = \text{rank}(A) \). This result is foundational for dimensionality reduction.

sections.title: Applications in ML
sections.body:
Application How SVD is used
PCA Right singular vectors of centered data matrix are the principal components; \( \sigma_i^2 / (N-1) \) gives the variance explained
Latent Semantic Analysis Truncated SVD on term-document matrix reveals latent semantic dimensions
Recommender systems Matrix factorization of user-item rating matrices; basis of Simon Funk's Netflix Prize approach
Pseudoinverse Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse computed via \( A^+ = V\Sigma^+ U^T \) where \( \Sigma^+ \) inverts non-zero singular values
Model compression Low-rank approximation of weight matrices in neural networks to reduce parameter count and computation

Computational cost: Full SVD is \( O(\min(m^2 n, mn^2)) \). Randomized SVD (Halko, Martinsson, Tropp, 2011) computes approximate truncated SVD in \( O(mn \log k) \) for rank-\( k \) approximation, making it practical for large-scale applications (used by scikit-learn's TruncatedSVD).

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Existence of SVD. Consider \( A^T A \), which is symmetric positive semi-definite. Its eigendecomposition gives \( A^T A = V \Lambda V^T \) with eigenvalues \( \lambda_i \geq 0 \). Define \( \sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i} \) and \( \Sigma = \text{diag}(\sigma_1, \ldots, \sigma_r) \). For each non-zero singular value, define \( u_i = \frac{1}{\sigma_i} A v_i \). These vectors are orthonormal:

\[ u_i^T u_j = \frac{1}{\sigma_i \sigma_j} v_i^T A^T A v_j = \frac{1}{\sigma_i \sigma_j} v_i^T \lambda_j v_j = \frac{\sigma_j}{\sigma_i}\delta_{ij} = \delta_{ij} \]

Extending \( \{u_i\} \) to a full orthonormal basis of \( \mathbb{R}^m \) gives \( U \), and we obtain \( A = U\Sigma V^T \).

title: Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) Estimation
summary: A point estimation method that finds the mode of the posterior distribution \( P(\theta|X) \), combining the likelihood of the data with a prior belief over parameters. MAP generalizes Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) by incorporating…

papers.citation: 1774 · Pierre-Simon Laplace · Mémoire sur la probabilité des causes par les événemens 2006 · Christopher Bishop · Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning

sections.body:
A point estimation method that finds the mode of the posterior distribution \( P(\theta|X) \), combining the likelihood of the data with a prior belief over parameters. MAP generalizes Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) by incorporating a prior that acts as a regularizer.

MAP estimation lies between MLE (no prior, purely data-driven) and full Bayesian inference (which computes the entire posterior). It provides a single point estimate that balances data fit with prior knowledge, and is computationally much cheaper than full Bayesian methods while still offering regularization benefits.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The posterior is a compromise between the prior distribution and the data likelihood. MAP estimation selects the mode of this posterior.

Derivation from Bayes' theorem. By Bayes' rule:

\[ P(\theta|X) = \frac{P(X|\theta)\,P(\theta)}{P(X)} \propto P(X|\theta)\,P(\theta) \]

Since \( P(X) \) does not depend on \( \theta \), the MAP estimate is:

\[ \hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_\theta\, P(X|\theta)\,P(\theta) = \arg\max_\theta\left[\log P(X|\theta) + \log P(\theta)\right] \]

The first term is the log-likelihood (same as in MLE). The second term, \( \log P(\theta) \), penalizes parameter values that are unlikely under the prior, acting as a regularizer.

sections.title: Prior-regularization equivalences
sections.body:
Prior on \( \theta \) Equivalent regularization Penalty form
\( \theta_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2) \) L2 / Ridge / Weight decay \( \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\|\theta\|_2^2 \)
\( \theta_j \sim \text{Laplace}(0, b) \) L1 / Lasso \( \frac{1}{b}\|\theta\|_1 \)
Uniform (improper) None (recovers MLE) Constant — no penalty

The Gaussian prior yields the L2 penalty because \( \log P(\theta) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_j \theta_j^2 + \text{const} \), which is exactly the Ridge penalty scaled by the prior precision \( 1/\sigma^2 \). The Laplace prior yields L1 because its log-density is \( -\frac{1}{b}|\theta_j| + \text{const} \), encouraging sparsity.

sections.title: Limitations compared to full Bayesian inference
sections.body:
• MAP gives a point estimate and provides no uncertainty quantification. Full Bayesian inference maintains the entire posterior \( P(\theta|X) \), enabling predictive distributions and credible intervals.
  • MAP is not invariant to reparameterization: if \( \phi = g(\theta) \), the MAP estimate of \( \phi \) is generally not \( g(\hat\theta_{\text{MAP}}) \), because the Jacobian of the transformation changes the location of the mode.
  • The posterior mode can be a poor summary of a skewed or multimodal posterior. The posterior mean (under squared loss) or median (under absolute loss) may be more representative.

Connections: MAP is widely used in practice: L2-regularized logistic regression, Ridge regression, and weight decay in neural networks can all be viewed as MAP estimation with Gaussian priors. The Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm can be adapted to compute MAP estimates (MAP-EM). In the limit of infinite data, MAP converges to MLE since the likelihood dominates the prior.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Gaussian prior yields L2 regularization. Let \( \theta_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2) \) independently. The log-prior is:

\[ \log P(\theta) = \sum_j \log \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\theta_j^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) = -\frac{d}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_j \theta_j^2 \]

Dropping constants (which don't affect the argmax):

\[ \hat\theta_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_\theta \left[\log P(X|\theta) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\|\theta\|_2^2\right] = \arg\min_\theta \left[-\log P(X|\theta) + \frac{\lambda}{2}\|\theta\|_2^2\right] \]

with \( \lambda = 1/\sigma^2 \). This is exactly the Ridge regression objective, showing that a tighter prior (smaller \( \sigma^2 \)) corresponds to stronger regularization.

title: Supervised learning
summary: A machine learning paradigm where a model is trained on input-output pairs with explicit labels, learning a mapping from inputs to targets that generalizes to unseen data. It encompasses both classification (discrete targets) and regress…

papers.citation: 1998 · Vladimir Vapnik · Statistical Learning Theory

sections.body:
A machine learning paradigm where a model is trained on input-output pairs with explicit labels, learning a mapping from inputs to targets that generalizes to unseen data. It encompasses both classification (discrete targets) and regression (continuous targets).

Supervised learning is the most widely used ML paradigm and forms the basis of most practical systems. The training dataset consists of labeled pairs \( \mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^N \), where \( x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d \) is an input feature vector and \( y_i \) is the corresponding target. The goal is to learn a function \( f_\theta: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathcal{Y} \) that minimizes expected loss on unseen data drawn from the same distribution.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The model predicts an output from input x. The loss function compares this prediction to the true label y, providing a signal to update model parameters.

Formal framework. The learning problem is formulated as empirical risk minimization (ERM):

\[ \hat\theta = \arg\min_\theta \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \ell(f_\theta(x_i),\, y_i) + \lambda\,\Omega(\theta) \]

where \( \ell \) is a loss function and \( \Omega(\theta) \) is an optional regularizer. The true objective is the expected risk \( R(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P}[\ell(f_\theta(x), y)] \), but since \( P \) is unknown, we minimize the empirical risk as a proxy.

sections.title: Common task-loss pairings
sections.body:
Task Output space \( \mathcal{Y} \) Typical loss Example models
Binary classification \( \{0, 1\} \) Binary cross-entropy / hinge loss Logistic regression, SVM
Multi-class classification \( \{1, \ldots, K\} \) Cross-entropy (softmax + NLL) Neural networks, Random Forests
Regression \( \mathbb{R} \) MSE / MAE / Huber Linear regression, Gradient Boosted Trees
Structured prediction Sequences, trees, graphs CTC, sequence cross-entropy CRFs, Seq2Seq, Transformers

sections.title: Key concepts and trade-offs
sections.body:
• Bias-variance trade-off: Simple models (high bias, low variance) underfit; complex models (low bias, high variance) overfit. Regularization, cross-validation, and early stopping help navigate this trade-off.
  • Generalization: PAC learning theory and VC dimension provide bounds on how well empirical risk minimization generalizes. Practically, generalization is assessed via held-out validation and test sets.
