  • Label cost: Supervised learning requires labeled data, which is expensive to obtain. This motivates semi-supervised learning (few labels + much unlabeled data), self-supervised learning (pretext tasks on unlabeled data), and active learning (strategically querying labels).

Connections: Supervised learning contrasts with unsupervised learning (no labels) and reinforcement learning (delayed reward signals). Many modern systems combine paradigms: e.g., self-supervised pretraining (unsupervised) followed by supervised fine-tuning, as in BERT and GPT.

title: Unsupervised learning
summary: A machine learning paradigm where models learn structure, patterns, or representations from data without explicit labels, including clustering, dimensionality reduction, density estimation, and generative modeling.

papers.citation: 2001 · Hastie, Tibshirani, Friedman · The Elements of Statistical Learning

sections.body:
A machine learning paradigm where models learn structure, patterns, or representations from data without explicit labels, including clustering, dimensionality reduction, density estimation, and generative modeling.

In unsupervised learning, the dataset consists only of inputs \( \mathcal{D} = \{x_i\}_{i=1}^N \) with no target labels. The objective varies by task but generally involves discovering the underlying data distribution \( p(x) \) or learning useful representations. Because there is no ground truth signal, evaluation is inherently more challenging than in supervised learning — metrics are often task-specific or rely on downstream performance.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Algorithms automatically identify underlying patterns, such as clustering related points or finding a manifold of lower dimensionality.

sections.title: Major unsupervised learning paradigms
sections.body:
Paradigm Objective Key methods
Clustering Partition data into groups with high intra-group similarity K-means (\( \min \sum_{k}\sum_{x \in C_k}\|x - \mu_k\|^2 \)), DBSCAN, Gaussian Mixture Models
Dimensionality reduction Find low-dimensional representations preserving structure PCA (projects onto top eigenvectors of covariance matrix), t-SNE, UMAP, autoencoders
Density estimation Estimate \( p(x) \) from samples Kernel Density Estimation, Gaussian Mixtures (EM algorithm), normalizing flows
Generative modeling Learn to sample new data from \( p(x) \) VAEs (\( \mathcal{L} = -\mathbb{E}_{q}[\log p(x|z)] + D_{\text{KL}}(q(z|x)\|p(z)) \)), GANs, diffusion models

Mathematical formulations. Many unsupervised methods can be cast as optimization problems:

  • PCA: Find the \( k \)-dimensional subspace maximizing variance: \( W^* = \arg\max_{W^TW=I} \text{tr}(W^T \Sigma W) \), where \( \Sigma \) is the data covariance matrix. The solution is the top \( k \) eigenvectors of \( \Sigma \), equivalently obtained via truncated SVD of the centered data matrix.
  • K-means: Minimize within-cluster sum of squares \( \sum_{k=1}^K \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \|x_i - \mu_k\|^2 \). This is NP-hard in general but Lloyd's algorithm provides a practical alternating minimization heuristic.
  • EM algorithm: For latent variable models like GMMs, iteratively computes expected sufficient statistics (E-step) and maximizes expected complete-data log-likelihood (M-step), guaranteeing monotonic increase of the marginal likelihood.

Self-supervised learning blurs the boundary between supervised and unsupervised: it creates surrogate labels from the data itself (e.g., predicting masked tokens in BERT, predicting the next token in GPT, contrastive learning in SimCLR). Technically unsupervised (no human labels), it uses a supervised-style loss and has become the dominant pretraining strategy for modern foundation models.

Connections: Unsupervised pretraining followed by supervised fine-tuning is a core recipe in modern deep learning. The representations learned by unsupervised methods (e.g., word embeddings, image features from autoencoders) transfer effectively to downstream supervised tasks. Reinforcement learning is a third distinct paradigm where the signal comes from environmental reward rather than data labels or structure.

title: Reinforcement learning (RL)
summary: A machine learning paradigm where an agent learns to make sequential decisions by interacting with an environment, receiving reward signals, and optimizing a policy to maximize cumulative long-term return.

papers.citation: 1989 · Christopher Watkins · Learning from Delayed Rewards (Q-learning) 2018 · Richard Sutton, Andrew Barto · Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction (2nd ed.)

sections.body:
A machine learning paradigm where an agent learns to make sequential decisions by interacting with an environment, receiving reward signals, and optimizing a policy to maximize cumulative long-term return.

Reinforcement learning (RL) differs fundamentally from supervised learning: there are no labeled input-output pairs. Instead, the agent must discover which actions yield the most reward through trial and error, balancing exploration (trying new actions) with exploitation (choosing known good actions). The field draws on control theory, operations research, and animal psychology (Thorndike's Law of Effect, 1911).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The fundamental RL loop: the agent takes an action in the environment, which transitions to a new state and emits a reward signal.

Markov Decision Process (MDP) formalism. RL problems are typically modeled as an MDP \( (\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma) \):

  • \( \mathcal{S} \) — state space
  • \( \mathcal{A} \) — action space
  • \( \mathcal{P}(s'|s, a) \) — transition dynamics (probability of next state given current state and action)
  • \( \mathcal{R}(s, a) \) — reward function
  • \( \gamma \in [0, 1) \) — discount factor controlling the trade-off between immediate and future rewards

The agent follows a policy \( \pi(a|s) \) and seeks to maximize the expected discounted return:

\[ G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} \]

Value functions are central to RL. The state-value function \( V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t | s_t = s] \) and action-value function \( Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t | s_t = s, a_t = a] \) satisfy the Bellman equations:

\[ V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a|s) \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s'|s,a)\left[\mathcal{R}(s,a) + \gamma\, V^\pi(s')\right] \] \[ Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathcal{R}(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s'|s,a) \sum_{a'} \pi(a'|s')\, Q^\pi(s',a') \]

sections.title: Major algorithm families
sections.body:
Family Approach Key algorithms
Value-based Learn \( Q^*(s,a) \), derive policy greedily Q-learning, DQN (Mnih et al., 2015), Double DQN
Policy-based Directly optimize \( \pi_\theta(a|s) \) via gradient ascent on expected return REINFORCE, PPO (Schulman et al., 2017)
Actor-Critic Combine value function (critic) with policy (actor) to reduce variance A2C/A3C, SAC, TD3
Model-based Learn environment dynamics \( \mathcal{P} \), plan using learned model Dyna-Q, MuZero (Schrittwieser et al., 2020), World Models

The policy gradient theorem (Sutton et al., 1999) provides the foundation for policy-based methods:

\[ \nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta}\!\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a|s)\, Q^{\pi_\theta}(s,a)\right] \]

Key challenges: sample efficiency (model-free methods require millions of interactions), credit assignment over long horizons, partial observability (POMDPs), reward shaping and sparse rewards, and sim-to-real transfer. RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) applies RL to fine-tune language models using human preference data as the reward signal, and is a key ingredient in ChatGPT, Claude, and other aligned LLMs.

title: Linear function
summary: A function satisfying additivity \( f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y) \) and homogeneity \( f(cx) = c\,f(x) \), representable as a matrix multiplication \( f(x) = Wx \). In deep learning, the term is often used loosely to include affine functions \…

papers.citation: 2009 · Gilbert Strang · Introduction to Linear Algebra

sections.body:
A function satisfying additivity \( f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y) \) and homogeneity \( f(cx) = c\,f(x) \), representable as a matrix multiplication \( f(x) = Wx \). In deep learning, the term is often used loosely to include affine functions \( f(x) = Wx + b \), though strictly these are not linear.

Linear functions (more precisely, linear maps or linear transformations) are the simplest parameterized functions and the foundational building block of neural networks. Every "layer" in a neural network applies a linear (or affine) transformation followed by a nonlinear activation.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: A linear transformation maps the input vector space to the output space while preserving straight lines and the origin.

Formal definition. A function \( f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m \) is linear if and only if it satisfies the superposition principle for all vectors \( x, y \in \mathbb{R}^n \) and scalars \( c \in \mathbb{R} \):

\[ f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y) \quad \text{(additivity)} \] \[ f(cx) = c\,f(x) \quad \text{(homogeneity)} \]

Equivalently, \( f(\alpha x + \beta y) = \alpha f(x) + \beta f(y) \). Every such function can be represented as \( f(x) = Wx \) for some matrix \( W \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n} \). A consequence is that \( f(0) = 0 \) — linear functions always map the origin to the origin.

Linear vs. affine. An affine function adds a bias: \( g(x) = Wx + b \), where \( b \in \mathbb{R}^m \). This does not satisfy homogeneity (since \( g(0) = b \neq 0 \) in general) and is therefore not linear in the strict sense. However, in deep learning the term "linear layer" almost always refers to the affine transformation \( Wx + b \). PyTorch's nn.Linear(n, m) implements the affine transform with \( mn + m \) parameters.

Why nonlinearity is essential. A composition of linear functions is still linear: if \( f_1(x) = W_1 x \) and \( f_2(x) = W_2 x \), then \( f_2(f_1(x)) = W_2 W_1 x = W' x \). This means a deep network of purely linear layers collapses to a single linear transformation, regardless of depth. Activation functions (ReLU, sigmoid, tanh, GELU) break this linearity, enabling neural networks to approximate arbitrary nonlinear functions (Universal Approximation Theorem).

sections.title: Properties of linear transformations
sections.body:
• Parameter count: A linear map from \( \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m \) has \( mn \) parameters (or \( mn + m \) with bias). For large layers, this dominates model size — e.g., a fully connected layer from 4096 to 4096 has ~16.8M parameters.
  • Gradient: The derivative of \( f(x) = Wx + b \) with respect to \( x \) is simply \( W \), and with respect to \( W \) is \( x^T \). This makes backpropagation through linear layers straightforward: \( \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}\, x^T \) and \( \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = W^T \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \).
  • Rank and expressiveness: The rank of \( W \) determines the dimensionality of the output space. Low-rank factorizations \( W \approx UV \) (where \( U \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times r}, V \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times n}, r \ll \min(m,n) \)) reduce parameter count from \( mn \) to \( r(m+n) \) and are the basis of LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) for fine-tuning large models.

sections.title: Role in neural architectures
sections.body:
• MLPs: Alternating linear layers and activations: \( h_l = \sigma(W_l h_{l-1} + b_l) \).
  • Transformers: The query/key/value projections (\( Q = XW_Q \), \( K = XW_K \), \( V = XW_V \)) and the feed-forward sub-layers are all linear (affine) transformations. Linear layers constitute the vast majority of parameters in a Transformer.
  • CNNs: Convolution is a structured linear operation (a Toeplitz matrix multiplication), and classification heads use fully connected linear layers.

title: Affine transformation
summary: A transformation composed of a linear map followed by a translation: \( f(\mathbf{x}) = W\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b} \). It is the fundamental computational unit inside every fully connected (dense) neural network layer.

papers.citation: 18th Century · Euler · Formalized in linear algebra and analytic geometry

sections.body:
A transformation composed of a linear map followed by a translation: \( f(\mathbf{x}) = W\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b} \). It is the fundamental computational unit inside every fully connected (dense) neural network layer.

Geometric interpretation: An affine transformation shears, scales, and translates the original space (blue square to red parallelogram).

An affine transformation maps an input vector \( \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \) to an output \( \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^m \) via:

\[ \mathbf{y} = W\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b} \]

where \( W \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n} \) is the weight matrix and \( \mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^m \) is the bias vector. Unlike a purely linear map (which must satisfy \( f(\mathbf{0}) = \mathbf{0} \)), the bias term shifts the output, allowing the transformation to model functions that do not pass through the origin.

Parameter count. A single affine layer mapping \( n \) inputs to \( m \) outputs has \( mn + m \) learnable parameters. For example, a layer mapping 512 to 256 units has \( 512 \times 256 + 256 = 131{,}328 \) parameters. This quadratic scaling in hidden size is why fully connected layers are parameter-expensive compared to convolutional layers.

Collapse property. Composing two affine transformations without a non-linearity yields another affine transformation:

\[ W_2(W_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1) + \mathbf{b}_2 = (W_2 W_1)\mathbf{x} + (W_2 \mathbf{b}_1 + \mathbf{b}_2) \]

This means a deep stack of purely affine layers has no more representational power than a single affine layer. Inserting non-linear activation functions (ReLU, GELU, sigmoid) between affine layers is what gives deep networks their expressive power, enabling them to approximate arbitrary continuous functions (Universal Approximation Theorem).

sections.title: Where affine layers appear
sections.body:
• MLPs: every hidden layer is an affine transformation followed by an activation.
  • CNNs: the classifier head typically uses one or more affine (fully connected) layers after the convolutional feature extractor.
  • Transformers: the position-wise feed-forward network in each block consists of two affine transformations with a non-linearity between them: \( \text{FFN}(\mathbf{x}) = W_2 \, \sigma(W_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1) + \mathbf{b}_2 \).

Geometric intuition. An affine transformation can perform rotation, scaling, shearing, reflection, and translation of the input space. Each layer of a neural network progressively reshapes the data geometry so that the final representation is linearly separable (for classification) or lies on the target manifold (for regression).

title: Loss function
summary: A scalar function \( \mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y) \) that quantifies the discrepancy between a model's prediction \( \hat{y} \) and the ground-truth target \( y \), providing the signal that drives parameter optimization.

papers.citation: 1805 · Legendre, Gauss · Least squares method

sections.body:
A scalar function \( \mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y) \) that quantifies the discrepancy between a model's prediction \( \hat{y} \) and the ground-truth target \( y \), providing the signal that drives parameter optimization.

The loss function maps parameter configurations to a scalar error. Optimization seeks the global minimum.

The loss function (also called objective, cost, or criterion) is the quantity that training seeks to minimize. Given model parameters \( \theta \), the empirical risk over a dataset of \( N \) examples is:

\[ J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\bigl(f_\theta(x_i),\; y_i\bigr) \]

Gradient-based optimizers compute \( \nabla_\theta J(\theta) \) and update parameters to reduce this quantity iteratively.

sections.title: Common loss functions
sections.body:
Loss Formula Use case
MSE \( \frac{1}{N}\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 \) Regression
MAE \( \frac{1}{N}\sum|y_i - \hat{y}_i| \) Robust regression
Cross-Entropy \( -\sum_c y_c \log \hat{y}_c \) Classification
Binary CE \( -[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})] \) Binary classification
Hinge \( \max(0, 1 - y \cdot \hat{y}) \) SVMs, margin-based

Probabilistic interpretation. Many losses correspond to maximum likelihood estimation under specific distributional assumptions. MSE is the MLE solution when errors are i.i.d. Gaussian; cross-entropy is the MLE for a categorical (softmax) output distribution. This connection to probability theory explains why different tasks require different losses: the loss implicitly specifies the noise model.

Regularized objectives. In practice, the total objective often includes an explicit regularization penalty: \( J_{\text{total}}(\theta) = J(\theta) + \lambda \, \Omega(\theta) \), where \( \Omega(\theta) \) might be L2 weight decay (\( \|\theta\|_2^2 \)) or L1 sparsity (\( \|\theta\|_1 \)). This trades off data fidelity against model complexity.

Desirable properties: a good loss function should be (1) differentiable almost everywhere (for gradient-based optimization), (2) bounded below (so a minimum exists), and (3) well-matched to the task's evaluation metric. Mismatches between training loss and evaluation metric (e.g., training with cross-entropy but evaluating with BLEU) are a persistent challenge in NLP and other domains.

title: Mean squared error (MSE)
summary: A regression loss function that computes the average squared error between predicted and actual values: \( \text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 \). Minimizing MSE is equivalent to maximum likelihood estimation under…

papers.citation: 1805 · Legendre, Gauss · Method of least squares

sections.body:
A regression loss function that computes the average squared error between predicted and actual values: \( \text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 \). Minimizing MSE is equivalent to maximum likelihood estimation under Gaussian noise.

MSE scales quadratically with the residual error, heavily penalizing large outliers compared to MAE.

Given \( N \) predictions \( \hat{y}_i \) and targets \( y_i \), the mean squared error is:

\[ \text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 \]

The gradient with respect to a single prediction is \( \frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{y}_i} \text{MSE} = -\frac{2}{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i) \), which is linear in the residual. This means large errors produce proportionally large gradients, making MSE sensitive to outliers.

Probabilistic derivation. If we assume targets are generated as \( y_i = f_\theta(x_i) + \epsilon_i \) where \( \epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2) \), the negative log-likelihood of the data is:

\[ -\log p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{X}, \theta) = \frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) + \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - f_\theta(x_i))^2 \]

Minimizing this with respect to \( \theta \) is equivalent to minimizing MSE, since the first term is constant. This is why MSE is the natural loss for regression with homoscedastic Gaussian noise.

sections.title: Comparison with other regression losses
sections.body:
Loss Gradient behavior Outlier sensitivity
MSE Linear in residual High (quadratic penalty)
MAE / L1 Constant magnitude Low (linear penalty)
Huber Linear near 0, constant far Moderate (best of both)

Practical notes. MSE is the default loss for regression in virtually all deep learning frameworks (nn.MSELoss in PyTorch, tf.keras.losses.MeanSquaredError). When targets have vastly different scales across dimensions, normalizing the targets or using a per-dimension weighting is important to prevent one dimension from dominating the loss.

Derivations

Starting from the Gaussian likelihood assumption:

\[ p(y_i \mid x_i, \theta) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(y_i - f_\theta(x_i))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \]

For \( N \) i.i.d. samples, the log-likelihood is:

\[ \log p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{X}, \theta) = -\frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - f_\theta(x_i))^2 \]

Maximizing this is equivalent to minimizing \( \sum(y_i - f_\theta(x_i))^2 \), i.e., MSE.

Python 
import numpy as np

def mse_loss(y_true, y_pred):
    """Mean Squared Error with gradient."""
    N = len(y_true)
    loss = np.mean((y_true - y_pred) ** 2)
    grad = -2 / N * (y_true - y_pred)  # dL/d(y_pred)
    return loss, grad

title: Prediction error
summary: The difference between a model's prediction and the true target value, \( e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i \). Also called the residual, it is the atomic quantity from which all loss functions are constructed.

papers.citation: 1800s · Gauss, Legendre · Foundational to the method of least squares

sections.body:
The difference between a model's prediction and the true target value, \( e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i \). Also called the residual, it is the atomic quantity from which all loss functions are constructed.

Prediction errors (residuals) are the vertical distances from the ground-truth observations to the model's predictive surface.

For a model \( f_\theta \) producing prediction \( \hat{y}_i = f_\theta(x_i) \), the prediction error (residual) for the \( i \)-th example is:

\[ e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i \]

Loss functions aggregate these residuals into a single scalar. Different aggregation strategies yield different losses:

Loss Aggregation of residuals
MSE \( \frac{1}{N}\sum e_i^2 \)
MAE \( \frac{1}{N}\sum |e_i| \)
Huber \( \frac{1}{N}\sum h_\delta(e_i) \) (quadratic near 0, linear beyond \( \delta \))

Diagnostic value. Analyzing the distribution and structure of prediction errors is a primary tool for model diagnosis:

  • Systematic bias (non-zero mean of residuals): indicates underfitting or a misspecified model that consistently over- or under-predicts.
  • Heteroscedasticity (variance of residuals changes with input magnitude): suggests the model should use a heteroscedastic loss or log-transform targets.
  • Heavy tails (large residuals more frequent than Gaussian): signals outliers or the need for a robust loss like MAE or Huber.
  • Autocorrelation in residuals (for time-series): indicates the model is not capturing temporal patterns.

Bias-variance decomposition. The expected prediction error for a model can be decomposed as:

\[ \mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{y})^2] = \text{Bias}[\hat{y}]^2 + \text{Var}[\hat{y}] + \sigma^2 \]

where \( \sigma^2 \) is irreducible noise. This decomposition explains the fundamental trade-off: reducing bias (more complex models) typically increases variance, and vice versa.

title: Gradient descent
summary: A first-order iterative optimization algorithm that minimizes an objective function by repeatedly stepping in the direction of the negative gradient: \( \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t) \).

papers.citation: 1847 · Augustin-Louis Cauchy

sections.body:
A first-order iterative optimization algorithm that minimizes an objective function by repeatedly stepping in the direction of the negative gradient: \( \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t) \).

Gradient descent iteratively updates parameters orthogonal to the loss contours to find the global minimum.

Given a differentiable loss function \( J(\theta) \), gradient descent updates the parameter vector \( \theta \) by moving opposite to the gradient (the direction of steepest ascent):

\[ \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \, \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t) \]

where \( \eta > 0 \) is the learning rate (step size) and \( \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t) \) is the gradient of the full loss over the entire training set of \( N \) examples.

Why it works. A first-order Taylor expansion gives \( J( heta + \Delta heta) \approx J( heta) + abla_ heta J( heta)^ op \Delta heta \). Choosing \( \Delta heta = -\eta abla_ heta J \) guarantees the loss decreases (for sufficiently small \( \eta \)), since the inner product \( -\eta \| abla_ heta J\|^2 < 0 \).

sections.title: Convergence properties
sections.body:
• For convex functions with Lipschitz-continuous gradients (constant \( L \)), GD with \( \eta \leq 1/L \) converges at rate \( O(1/T) \).
  • For strongly convex functions (condition number \( \kappa = L/\mu \)), convergence is linear: \( O\!\left(\left(1 - 1/\kappa\right)^T\right) \).
  • For non-convex functions (typical in deep learning), GD converges to a stationary point (\( \nabla J \approx 0 \)) but with no guarantee it is a global minimum.

Learning rate sensitivity. If \( \eta \) is too large, the updates overshoot and the loss diverges. If too small, convergence is impractically slow. The optimal rate depends on the local curvature (Hessian eigenvalues), motivating adaptive methods like Adam that maintain per-parameter rates.

sections.title: Variants and extensions
sections.body:
Variant Batch size Key trade-off
Full-batch GD \( N \) (all data) Exact gradient, slow per step
SGD 1 Noisy gradient, fast per step, implicit regularization
Mini-batch GD \( b \) (e.g. 32-512) Variance reduction + GPU parallelism

Connection to second-order methods. Newton's method uses the Hessian \( H \) to compute \( \Delta\theta = -H^{-1}\nabla J \), which accounts for curvature and converges quadratically. However, computing and inverting the Hessian is \( O(d^3) \) for \( d \) parameters, making it infeasible for modern neural networks with millions of parameters. Quasi-Newton methods (L-BFGS) approximate the Hessian cheaply and are used for small-to-medium-scale problems.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
For \( J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N}\|X\theta - y\|^2 \):

\[ \nabla_\theta J = \frac{1}{N} X^\top(X\theta - y) \]

The update becomes: \( \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{N} X^\top(X\theta_t - y) \).

sections.title: Python
sections.body:
import numpy as np

def gradient_descent(X, y, theta, lr=0.01, epochs=1000, tol=1e-6):
    """Full-batch gradient descent for linear regression."""
    N = len(y)
    losses = []
    for t in range(epochs):
        preds = X.dot(theta)
        loss = np.mean((preds - y) ** 2)
        losses.append(loss)
        grad = (2 / N) * X.T.dot(preds - y)
        theta = theta - lr * grad
        if np.linalg.norm(grad) < tol:
            break
    return theta, losses

title: Mini-batch gradient descent
summary: A variant of gradient descent that computes the gradient over a small, randomly sampled subset (mini-batch) of the training data at each step, balancing the exact gradient of full-batch GD against the high variance of single-sample SGD.

papers.citation: 2012+ · Popularized by deep learning practice · See: Goyal et al., "Accurate, Large Minibatch SGD"

sections.body:
A variant of gradient descent that computes the gradient over a small, randomly sampled subset (mini-batch) of the training data at each step, balancing the exact gradient of full-batch GD against the high variance of single-sample SGD.

Mini-batch GD paths are noisier than full-batch GD due to estimation variance, but step significantly faster in wall-clock time.

At each iteration, a mini-batch \( \mathcal{B} \subset \{1, \dots, N\} \) of size \( b \) is sampled (typically without replacement within an epoch), and the gradient is estimated as:

\[ g_t = \frac{1}{b} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \nabla_\theta \ell\bigl(f_\theta(x_i),\, y_i\bigr) \]

The parameter update is then \( \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \, g_t \). This estimate is unbiased: \( \mathbb{E}[g_t] = \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t) \), meaning it equals the true full-batch gradient in expectation.

Variance reduction. The variance of the mini-batch gradient estimate decreases as \( O(1/b) \):

\[ \text{Var}[g_t] = \frac{1}{b}\,\text{Var}\bigl[\nabla_\theta \ell_i\bigr] \]

Increasing batch size reduces noise but with diminishing returns, and very large batches can hurt generalization (the "generalization gap" observed by Keskar et al., 2017).

Hardware efficiency. Modern GPUs/TPUs are designed for parallel matrix operations. A batch of 32-512 examples can be processed in roughly the same wall-clock time as a single example due to SIMD parallelism. This makes mini-batch GD dramatically faster than pure SGD in practice. The optimal batch size is typically the largest that fits in accelerator memory while still generalizing well.

sections.title: Typical batch sizes and their effects
sections.body:
Batch size Gradient noise GPU utilization Generalization
1 (SGD) Very high Poor Good (implicit regularization)
32 – 256 Moderate Good Good
512 – 4096 Low High Can degrade without LR scaling
\( > \)8192 Very low Saturated Often worse; needs warmup + LARS/LAMB

Linear scaling rule. When increasing batch size by factor \( k \), the learning rate should also be scaled by \( k \) (Goyal et al., 2017). With very large batches, a gradual warmup phase prevents early training instability. Optimizers like LARS and LAMB were designed specifically for large-batch training.

Epoch structure. One pass through all \( N \) examples constitutes an epoch. Within each epoch, data is typically shuffled and partitioned into \( \lceil N/b \rceil \) non-overlapping mini-batches. This without-replacement sampling reduces variance compared to sampling with replacement.

Python 
import numpy as np

def minibatch_gd(X, y, theta, lr=0.01, batch_size=32, epochs=100):
    """Mini-batch gradient descent for linear regression."""
    N = len(y)
    for epoch in range(epochs):
        indices = np.random.permutation(N)
        for start in range(0, N, batch_size):
            batch_idx = indices[start:start + batch_size]
            X_b, y_b = X[batch_idx], y[batch_idx]
            grad = (2 / len(y_b)) * X_b.T.dot(X_b.dot(theta) - y_b)
            theta = theta - lr * grad
    return theta

title: Stochastic gradient descent (SGD)
summary: An optimization algorithm that approximates the true gradient by computing it from a single randomly selected training example at each step: \( \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \ell(f_\theta(x_i), y_i) \). The high variance o…

papers.citation: 1951 · Robbins, Monro · "A Stochastic Approximation Method"

sections.body:
An optimization algorithm that approximates the true gradient by computing it from a single randomly selected training example at each step: \( \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \ell(f_\theta(x_i), y_i) \). The high variance of these updates provides implicit regularization.

SGD computes gradients from single examples, producing a highly erratic path. This injected noise acts as an implicit regularizer.

In full-batch gradient descent, the gradient is computed over all \( N \) training examples, which is expensive. SGD instead samples a single example \( (x_i, y_i) \) uniformly at random and performs the update:

\[ \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \, \nabla_\theta \ell\bigl(f_\theta(x_i),\, y_i\bigr) \]

This per-sample gradient is an unbiased estimator of the true gradient: \( \mathbb{E}_i[\nabla_\theta \ell_i] = \nabla_\theta J(\theta) \), but has high variance since it is based on a single data point.

Convergence theory. The Robbins-Monro conditions guarantee convergence if the learning rate schedule satisfies:

\[ \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_t = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_t^2 < \infty \]

A common schedule satisfying these conditions is \( \eta_t = \eta_0 / (1 + \alpha t) \). For convex functions, SGD converges at rate \( O(1/\sqrt{T}) \), which is slower than full-batch GD's \( O(1/T) \) but each iteration is \( N \times \) cheaper, making overall wall-clock time much lower.

Implicit regularization. The noise injected by random sampling helps SGD escape sharp local minima and saddle points, biasing convergence toward flatter minima that tend to generalize better. This has been formalized by viewing SGD as approximate Bayesian inference (Mandt et al., 2017) and through the lens of stochastic differential equations, where SGD approximates Langevin dynamics with a noise term proportional to the learning rate.

Momentum. Plain SGD is rarely used alone in practice. SGD with momentum (Polyak, 1964) accumulates an exponentially weighted moving average of past gradients:

\[ v_t = \beta v_{t-1} + \nabla_\theta \ell_i, \quad \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \, v_t \]

where \( \beta \approx 0.9 \). This dampens oscillations across high-curvature directions and accelerates progress along consistent gradient directions. Nesterov momentum computes the gradient at a "look-ahead" position \( \theta_t - \eta \beta v_{t-1} \) for improved convergence.

sections.title: SGD vs. adaptive methods
sections.body:
Property SGD + momentum Adam
Tuning difficulty High (LR sensitive) Low (robust defaults)
Final generalization Often better Sometimes worse
Convergence speed Slower initially Faster initially

In practice, SGD with momentum remains the preferred optimizer for training image classifiers (ResNets, etc.) and often achieves better test accuracy than Adam, though Adam is preferred for Transformers and NLP tasks where its adaptive rates handle sparse gradients well.

Python 
import numpy as np

def sgd(X, y, theta, lr=0.01, epochs=10, momentum=0.9):
    """SGD with optional momentum for linear regression."""
    N = len(y)
    v = np.zeros_like(theta)
    for epoch in range(epochs):
        indices = np.random.permutation(N)
        for i in indices:
            xi = X[i:i+1]
            yi = y[i:i+1]
            grad = 2 * xi.T.dot(xi.dot(theta) - yi)
            v = momentum * v + grad
            theta = theta - lr * v
    return theta

title: Convergence
summary: The property that an optimization algorithm reaches a point where the loss stabilizes and parameters cease to change significantly, formally when \( \nabla_\theta J(\theta) \to 0 \).

papers.citation: 19th Century · Cauchy (gradient descent), Robbins & Monro (stochastic convergence)

sections.body:
The property that an optimization algorithm reaches a point where the loss stabilizes and parameters cease to change significantly, formally when \( \nabla_\theta J(\theta) \to 0 \).

Convergence is reached when the loss plateaus. Practitioners monitor validation loss to halt training before overfitting begins.

Convergence means the iterative optimization process has found a stationary point of the loss function. Formally, a point \( \theta^* \) is stationary if \( \nabla_\theta J(\theta^*) = 0 \). In the landscape of possible solutions, stationary points can be:

  • Global minimum: the lowest possible loss value. Guaranteed to be found only for convex objectives.
  • Local minimum: lower than all nearby points but potentially far from the global minimum.
  • Saddle point: a point where the gradient is zero but it is a minimum along some directions and a maximum along others. In high-dimensional spaces (typical of deep learning), saddle points vastly outnumber local minima (Dauphin et al., 2014).

sections.title: Convergence rates for gradient descent
sections.body:
Function class GD rate SGD rate
Convex, Lipschitz gradient \( O(1/T) \) \( O(1/\sqrt{T}) \)
Strongly convex (\( \kappa = L/\mu \)) \( O(e^{-T/\kappa}) \) (linear) \( O(1/T) \)
Non-convex (deep nets) \( O(1/\sqrt{T}) \) to stationary \( O(1/\sqrt{T}) \) to stationary

Practical convergence in deep learning. True convergence to a global minimum is neither achievable nor necessary for deep networks. Instead, practitioners monitor convergence heuristically:

  • Training loss plateau: the training loss flattens and oscillates within a small band.
  • Validation loss monitoring: training is stopped when validation loss stops improving, which is early stopping — itself a form of regularization.
  • Gradient norm: \( \|\nabla_\theta J\| \) approaches a small threshold.

Learning rate and convergence. The learning rate \( \eta \) directly controls whether and how fast convergence occurs. For a quadratic loss with Hessian eigenvalues in \( [\mu, L] \), convergence requires \( \eta < 2/L \). The optimal fixed rate is \( \eta^* = 2/(\mu + L) \). Modern learning rate schedules (cosine annealing, linear warmup + decay) are designed to ensure rapid initial progress while still converging to a good solution.

Loss landscape geometry. Recent work (Li et al., 2018) visualizes loss landscapes and shows that skip connections (as in ResNets and Transformers) dramatically smooth the loss surface, making convergence easier and less sensitive to initialization. Without skip connections, the loss surface is highly chaotic with many sharp local minima.

title: Generalization
summary: A model's ability to perform accurately on unseen data drawn from the same distribution as the training set. Generalization is the central goal of supervised learning — a model that memorizes training data but fails on new data is useles…

papers.citation: 1971 · Vapnik, Chervonenkis · "On the Uniform Convergence of Relative Frequencies of Events to Their Probabilities"

sections.body:
A model's ability to perform accurately on unseen data drawn from the same distribution as the training set. Generalization is the central goal of supervised learning — a model that memorizes training data but fails on new data is useless in practice.

The classical bias-variance trade-off illustrates how increasing capacity minimizes underfitting (bias) but eventually worsens generalization by overfitting (variance).

Generalization error is the difference between the model's expected loss on the true data distribution \( p(x, y) \) and its empirical loss on the training set:

\[ \text{Generalization gap} = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim p}[\ell(f_\theta(x), y)] - \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\ell(f_\theta(x_i), y_i) \]

A small training loss with a large generalization gap indicates overfitting; a high training loss indicates underfitting.

Bias-variance trade-off. For squared error loss, the expected prediction error decomposes as:

\[ \mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{y})^2] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}[\hat{y}]^2}_{\text{systematic error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}[\hat{y}]}_{\text{sensitivity to training set}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible noise}} \]

Simple models (few parameters) have high bias but low variance; complex models have low bias but high variance. The sweet spot minimizes total error. However, modern deep networks challenge this classical picture — they can have zero training error yet still generalize well (the "double descent" phenomenon).

PAC learning and VC theory. Vapnik-Chervonenkis theory provides distribution-free bounds on generalization error. For a hypothesis class with VC dimension \( d_{\text{VC}} \), with probability \( 1 - \delta \):

\[ \text{Generalization gap} \leq O\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{d_{\text{VC}} \log(N/d_{\text{VC}}) + \log(1/\delta)}{N}}\right) \]

This bound says generalization improves with more data (\( N \)) and degrades with model complexity (\( d_{\text{VC}} \)). However, VC bounds are often vacuous for modern deep networks (where \( d_{\text{VC}} \gg N \)), suggesting that classical theory does not fully explain deep learning generalization.

sections.title: Techniques that improve generalization
sections.body:
• Regularization: L2/L1 penalties, dropout, weight decay constrain model complexity.
  • Data augmentation: synthetically expanding the training distribution.
  • Early stopping: halting training when validation loss increases, limiting effective model complexity.
  • Ensemble methods: averaging predictions from multiple models reduces variance.
  • Cross-validation: \( k \)-fold CV provides a better estimate of generalization error than a single held-out set.

Double descent. Belkin et al. (2019) showed that test error follows a "double descent" curve: it first decreases, then increases near the interpolation threshold (where the model has just enough capacity to fit the training data), then decreases again as the model becomes highly overparameterized. This contradicts the classical U-shaped bias-variance curve and suggests that overparameterization can be beneficial.

title: Regularization
summary: A family of techniques that constrain or modify the learning process to reduce overfitting and improve generalization, typically by penalizing model complexity or injecting noise during training.

papers.citation: 1943 · Tikhonov · Tikhonov regularization (ridge regression)

sections.body:
A family of techniques that constrain or modify the learning process to reduce overfitting and improve generalization, typically by penalizing model complexity or injecting noise during training.

Geometric intuition for regularization. The sharp corners of the L1 penalty (left) cause intersections on the axes, driving specific parameters exactly to zero (sparsity). L2 (right) shrinks all parameters uniformly.

Regularization addresses the fundamental problem that a model with sufficient capacity can memorize the training set perfectly while failing on unseen data. All regularization methods work by restricting the effective hypothesis space, either explicitly through the objective function or implicitly through the training procedure.

Explicit regularization adds a penalty term \( \Omega(\theta) \) to the loss function:

\[ J_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\ell(f_\theta(x_i), y_i) + \lambda\,\Omega(\theta) \]

where \( \lambda > 0 \) controls the regularization strength. Common penalty terms:

Method Penalty \( \Omega(\theta) \) Effect on weights
L2 (Ridge / weight decay) \( \frac{1}{2}\|\theta\|_2^2 = \frac{1}{2}\sum \theta_j^2 \) Shrinks all weights toward zero; no exact zeros
L1 (Lasso) \( \|\theta\|_1 = \sum |\theta_j| \) Induces sparsity; drives some weights exactly to zero
Elastic Net \( \alpha\|\theta\|_1 + (1-\alpha)\frac{1}{2}\|\theta\|_2^2 \) Combines sparsity with grouping effect

Bayesian interpretation. L2 regularization is equivalent to placing a Gaussian prior \( p(\theta) = \mathcal{N}(0, \frac{1}{\lambda}I) \) on the weights and performing maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimation. L1 corresponds to a Laplace prior, which has heavier tails and more mass at zero, explaining why it promotes sparsity.

Implicit regularization does not alter the loss function directly but modifies the training dynamics to favor simpler solutions:

  • Dropout (Srivastava et al., 2014): randomly zeroes each neuron's activation with probability \( p \) during training, forcing the network to learn redundant representations. At test time, activations are scaled by \( (1-p) \). Equivalent to training an exponentially large ensemble of thinned networks.
  • Data augmentation: applies random transformations (flips, crops, color jitter, mixup) to training examples, effectively expanding the training distribution and teaching invariances.
  • Early stopping: halting training when validation loss begins to increase. Equivalent to L2 regularization in certain settings (Bishop, 1995), with the number of training iterations playing the role of inverse regularization strength.
  • SGD noise: the gradient noise from mini-batch sampling acts as an implicit regularizer, biasing the optimizer toward flat minima that generalize better.
  • Batch normalization: the noise from estimating batch statistics provides a mild regularization effect, which is why BN reduces the need for dropout.

Structural regularization constrains the architecture itself: weight sharing in CNNs (each filter has far fewer parameters than a fully connected layer), skip connections (which smooth the loss landscape), and parameter-efficient architectures like depthwise separable convolutions all reduce effective model complexity.

Choosing \( \lambda \). The regularization hyperparameter is typically selected by cross-validation or monitoring the validation loss. Too much regularization causes underfitting (high bias); too little allows overfitting (high variance).

title: L1 regularization (Lasso)
summary: L1 regularization adds the sum of absolute parameter values to the loss function, penalizing model complexity and driving many weights exactly to zero. This induces sparsity, making L1 an implicit feature-selection mechanism.

papers.citation: 1996 · Robert Tibshirani · Regression Shrinkage and Selection via the Lasso

sections.body:
L1 regularization adds the sum of absolute parameter values to the loss function, penalizing model complexity and driving many weights exactly to zero. This induces sparsity, making L1 an implicit feature-selection mechanism.

The regularized objective augments the base loss with a penalty scaled by \( \lambda > 0 \):

\[ J_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = J(\theta) + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} |\theta_i| \]

where \( \theta_i \) are the model parameters and \( \lambda \) controls the penalty strength. Because the L1 norm is not differentiable at zero, optimization typically uses subgradient methods or proximal gradient descent. The subgradient of \( |\theta_i| \) is:

\[ \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_i} |\theta_i| = \begin{cases} +1 & \theta_i > 0 \\ -1 & \theta_i < 0 \\ [-1, +1] & \theta_i = 0 \end{cases} \]

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Loss contours hit the L1 constraint diamond at a corner, forcing w₁ = 0.

Geometric intuition. The L1 constraint region \( \sum |\theta_i| \leq t \) forms a diamond (cross-polytope) in parameter space. The sharp vertices of this diamond lie on the coordinate axes, so the elliptical contours of the loss function are far more likely to make first contact at a vertex, setting one or more parameters exactly to zero. Compare this with L2 regularization, whose circular constraint region produces no such vertex effect and merely shrinks weights toward zero without eliminating them.

Sparsity and feature selection. The zero-driving property is what makes Lasso valuable beyond mere regularization: in a linear model with 1000 features, Lasso may select only 20 nonzero coefficients, yielding an interpretable model. This is critical in genomics, NLP feature engineering, and any domain where \( p \gg n \) (more features than samples).

Property L1 (Lasso) L2 (Ridge)
Penalty term\( \lambda \sum |\theta_i| \)\( \lambda \sum \theta_i^2 \)
Constraint shapeDiamond (cross-polytope)Sphere (hypersphere)
Produces exact zeros?YesNo
Feature selection?ImplicitNo

Elastic Net. In practice, pure L1 can be unstable when features are correlated (it arbitrarily selects one from a correlated group). Elastic Net (Zou & Hastie, 2005) combines L1 and L2 penalties: \( \lambda_1 \sum |\theta_i| + \lambda_2 \sum \theta_i^2 \), retaining sparsity while grouping correlated features together.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Why L1 induces sparsity (coordinate descent view). Consider minimizing \( f(\theta_i) = \frac{1}{2}(\theta_i - \hat{\theta}_i)^2 + \lambda |\theta_i| \) for a single coordinate, where \( \hat{\theta}_i \) is the OLS estimate. The solution is the soft-thresholding operator:

\[ \theta_i^* = \text{sign}(\hat{\theta}_i) \max(|\hat{\theta}_i| - \lambda, 0) \]

When \( |\hat{\theta}_i| < \lambda \), the parameter is driven exactly to zero. This closed-form solution underlies the coordinate descent algorithm for Lasso and directly shows the sparsity mechanism.

title: L2 regularization (Ridge/Weight Decay)
summary: L2 regularization adds the sum of squared parameter values to the loss function, uniformly shrinking all weights toward zero without eliminating any. In vanilla SGD, L2 regularization is mathematically equivalent to weight decay.

papers.citation: 1943 · Andrey Tikhonov · On the Stability of Inverse Problems

sections.body:
L2 regularization adds the sum of squared parameter values to the loss function, uniformly shrinking all weights toward zero without eliminating any. In vanilla SGD, L2 regularization is mathematically equivalent to weight decay.

The regularized objective is:

\[ J_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = J(\theta) + \frac{\lambda}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \theta_i^2 \]

where \( \lambda > 0 \) controls penalty strength and the factor of \( \frac{1}{2} \) is conventional for clean gradient expressions. The gradient of the penalty term is simply \( \lambda \theta_i \), yielding the update rule:

\[ \theta_i \leftarrow \theta_i - \eta \left( \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_i} + \lambda \theta_i \right) = (1 - \eta \lambda)\,\theta_i - \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_i} \]

The multiplicative factor \( (1 - \eta \lambda) \) shrinks every weight by a constant fraction each step, which is why the technique is called weight decay.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Loss contours hit the L2 constraint sphere smoothly, shrinking both weights without enforcing exact zeros.

Geometric intuition. The L2 constraint region \( \sum \theta_i^2 \leq t \) is a hypersphere. Because it has no corners, the loss contours almost never intersect the constraint boundary at a coordinate axis, so weights shrink but do not reach exactly zero. This contrasts with L1's diamond geometry, which promotes sparsity.

Bayesian interpretation. L2 regularization corresponds to placing a Gaussian prior \( \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2) \) on the parameters, where \( \lambda = 1/\sigma^2 \). MAP estimation under this prior yields the Ridge objective. L1 analogously corresponds to a Laplace prior.

Weight decay vs. L2 in adaptive optimizers. For vanilla SGD, L2 regularization and weight decay are identical. However, for adaptive methods like Adam, they diverge: L2 regularization scales the penalty by the adaptive learning rate, while decoupled weight decay (AdamW, Loshchilov & Hutter 2019) applies the decay directly to the weight, independent of the gradient moment estimates. AdamW consistently outperforms Adam + L2 in practice.

Aspect L2 Regularization Decoupled Weight Decay
Gradient term\( \nabla J + \lambda \theta \)\( \nabla J \) (decay applied separately)
Interacts with adaptive LR?YesNo
Used inSGD + L2, Adam + L2AdamW

Effect on eigenspectrum. In linear regression, Ridge adds \( \lambda I \) to the Gram matrix \( X^T X \), ensuring invertibility and shrinking the contribution of small-eigenvalue directions. This is the original Tikhonov regularization used to solve ill-posed inverse problems.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Ridge regression closed form. For the linear model \( y = X\theta + \epsilon \), the Ridge estimator is:

\[ \hat{\theta}_{\text{ridge}} = (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T y \]

Derived by setting the gradient of \( \|y - X\theta\|^2 + \lambda \|\theta\|^2 \) to zero:

\[ \nabla_\theta = -2X^T(y - X\theta) + 2\lambda\theta = 0 \]

\[ (X^T X + \lambda I)\theta = X^T y \]

The addition of \( \lambda I \) ensures the matrix is always invertible, even when \( X^T X \) is singular (more features than samples).

title: Early stopping
summary: Early stopping halts training when validation performance stops improving, using the model weights from the best-performing epoch. It is one of the simplest and most effective regularization techniques in deep learning.

papers.citation: 1995 · Sjöberg, Ljung; Bishop · Nonlinear Black-Box Modeling in System Identification

sections.body:
Early stopping halts training when validation performance stops improving, using the model weights from the best-performing epoch. It is one of the simplest and most effective regularization techniques in deep learning.

Mechanism. During training, the model is periodically evaluated on a held-out validation set. A patience counter tracks how many consecutive evaluations have passed without improvement. When patience is exhausted, training stops and the checkpoint with the lowest validation loss is restored:

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Training is stopped after the validation loss worsens sequentially for a defined "patience" window.

1. After each epoch (or evaluation interval), compute validation loss \( \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}^{(t)} \).
  2. If \( \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}^{(t)} < \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}^{(\text{best})} - \delta \), save the checkpoint and reset the patience counter. The tolerance \( \delta \geq 0 \) prevents stopping on trivial improvements.
  3. Otherwise, increment the patience counter. If it exceeds the threshold \( P \), stop training.

Connection to L2 regularization. Bishop (1995) and Sjöberg & Ljung (1995) showed that for quadratic loss surfaces, early stopping implicitly constrains the effective number of parameters. Starting from small random weights, gradient descent explores an expanding volume of parameter space over time. Stopping early limits this volume, functioning analogously to an L2 penalty. Specifically, for a linear model with learning rate \( \eta \) and \( t \) steps, the effective regularization parameter is approximately \( \lambda_{\text{eff}} \approx \frac{1}{\eta \cdot t} \).

sections.title: Practical considerations.
sections.body:
• Patience: Typical values range from 5 to 20 epochs. Too low risks stopping during temporary validation plateaus; too high wastes compute.
  • Metric choice: Usually validation loss, but can be any metric (accuracy, F1, BLEU). For metrics where higher is better, the comparison is inverted.
  • Interaction with learning rate schedules: Cosine annealing or warm restarts can cause temporary validation loss spikes, so patience must account for the schedule period.
  • No hyperparameter on model capacity: Unlike L1/L2 regularization, early stopping does not modify the loss function. It regularizes purely through optimization dynamics, making it compatible with any other regularization method.
Hyperparameter Typical range Effect
Patience \( P \)5–20 epochsHigher = more robust to noise, longer training
Tolerance \( \delta \)0–1e-4Ignores negligible improvements
Eval frequencyEvery epoch or every N stepsTrade-off between granularity and overhead
Python 
class EarlyStopping:
    def __init__(self, patience=10, min_delta=0.0):
        self.patience = patience
        self.min_delta = min_delta
        self.best_loss = float('inf')
        self.counter = 0
        self.best_weights = None

    def step(self, val_loss, model):
        if val_loss < self.best_loss - self.min_delta:
            self.best_loss = val_loss
            self.counter = 0
            self.best_weights = model.state_dict().copy()
        else:
            self.counter += 1
        return self.counter >= self.patience

    def restore(self, model):
        model.load_state_dict(self.best_weights)

title: Hyperparameter
summary: A hyperparameter is a configuration value set before training begins that governs the learning process or model architecture, as opposed to model parameters (weights, biases) which are learned from data via optimization.

papers.citation: 2012 · Bergstra, Bengio · Random Search for Hyper-Parameter Optimization

sections.body:
A hyperparameter is a configuration value set before training begins that governs the learning process or model architecture, as opposed to model parameters (weights, biases) which are learned from data via optimization.

Hyperparameters vs. parameters. Model parameters live inside the hypothesis space and are optimized by the training algorithm (e.g., gradient descent updates weights \( \theta \)). Hyperparameters sit outside this loop: they define the search space, the optimizer's behavior, or the model's structural capacity. Changing a hyperparameter typically requires restarting training from scratch.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Hyperparameters set the bounds and rules; parameters are the internal values searched during training.

Category Examples What it controls
OptimizerLearning rate \( \eta \), momentum \( \beta \), weight decay \( \lambda \)Step size, gradient smoothing, regularization
TrainingBatch size, number of epochs, warmup stepsData throughput, convergence dynamics
ArchitectureHidden layers, units per layer, attention headsModel capacity and inductive bias
RegularizationDropout rate, early stopping patienceOverfitting control

Hyperparameter optimization (HPO). Because the validation loss is a non-differentiable, noisy, and expensive function of hyperparameters, specialized search methods are used:

  • Grid search: Exhaustive evaluation over a predefined grid. Cost is \( O(k^d) \) for \( k \) values per dimension and \( d \) hyperparameters — intractable beyond 2–3 dimensions.
  • Random search (Bergstra & Bengio, 2012): Samples hyperparameters uniformly at random. Provably more efficient than grid search when only a few hyperparameters matter, because it does not waste budget on unimportant dimensions.
  • Bayesian optimization: Builds a probabilistic surrogate model (typically a Gaussian process) of the objective \( f(\mathbf{h}) \) and selects the next point to evaluate by maximizing an acquisition function (e.g., Expected Improvement). Much more sample-efficient than random search for expensive evaluations.
  • Hyperband / ASHA: Early-stopping-based methods that allocate more resources to promising configurations and kill underperformers. Hyperband frames HPO as a multi-armed bandit problem over resource allocation.

Sensitivity and interactions. Learning rate is almost universally the most critical hyperparameter. Batch size and learning rate interact strongly: the linear scaling rule (Goyal et al., 2017) suggests scaling \( \eta \propto B \) when increasing batch size \( B \). Modern large-scale training often fixes most hyperparameters and tunes only learning rate, warmup, and weight decay.

title: Data leakage
summary: Data leakage occurs when information from outside the training set — typically from the test set or from the future — inadvertently influences model building, producing inflated performance estimates that collapse in production.

papers.citation: 2011 · Kaufman et al. · Leakage in Data Mining: Formulation, Detection, and Avoidance

sections.body:
Data leakage occurs when information from outside the training set — typically from the test set or from the future — inadvertently influences model building, producing inflated performance estimates that collapse in production.

Why it matters. Leakage is one of the most common and damaging errors in applied ML. A model trained with leaked information appears to generalize well on validation metrics but fails on genuinely unseen data, because it has effectively memorized signals that will not be available at inference time.

sections.title: Illustration
Common forms of leakage
sections.body:
Type Mechanism Example
Preprocessing leakageFitting transformations on the full dataset before splittingComputing mean/std for standardization using train + test data
Target leakageIncluding features that are proxies for or derived from the labelUsing "treatment outcome" as a feature when predicting "will patient be treated"
Temporal leakageUsing future data to predict past eventsTraining a stock predictor on data shuffled across time, so tomorrow's features leak into today's prediction
Group leakageCorrelated samples span train and testMultiple CT slices from the same patient appear in both splits

sections.title: Detection strategies
sections.body:
• Suspiciously high metrics: If a model achieves near-perfect accuracy on a hard task, leakage is a likely culprit.
  • Feature importance analysis: If an unexpected feature dominates, inspect whether it encodes the target.
  • Adversarial validation: Train a classifier to distinguish train from test sets. If it succeeds (AUC > 0.5), the distributions differ — possibly due to leakage in the split.
  • Temporal consistency checks: Verify that all features for a sample were available before the prediction timestamp.

Prevention. The core principle is strict temporal and informational separation. Use sklearn.pipeline.Pipeline to ensure all preprocessing is fitted only on training folds. For time-series, always split chronologically. For grouped data (e.g., medical imaging), use GroupKFold to keep all samples from the same group in a single fold.

title: Backpropagation
summary: Backpropagation is the algorithm that computes the gradient of a scalar loss function with respect to every weight in a neural network by applying the chain rule layer-by-layer from output to input. It is the computational engine behind…

papers.citation: 1986 · Rumelhart, Hinton, Williams · Learning Representations by Back-Propagating Errors

sections.body:
Backpropagation is the algorithm that computes the gradient of a scalar loss function with respect to every weight in a neural network by applying the chain rule layer-by-layer from output to input. It is the computational engine behind gradient-based learning in deep networks.

Setup. Consider a network with \( L \) layers. Layer \( l \) computes pre-activation \( z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)} \) and activation \( a^{(l)} = \sigma(z^{(l)}) \), where \( a^{(0)} = x \) (the input) and \( \hat{y} = a^{(L)} \) (the output). The scalar loss is \( \mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y) \).

The backward recursion. Define the error signal at layer \( l \) as \( \delta^{(l)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l)}} \). Backpropagation computes this recursively:

\[ \delta^{(L)} = \nabla_{a^{(L)}} \mathcal{L} \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)}) \]

\[ \delta^{(l)} = \left( (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \right) \odot \sigma'(z^{(l)}) \]

where \( \odot \) denotes the Hadamard (element-wise) product. The parameter gradients then follow directly:

\[ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T, \qquad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} \]

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The error signal \( \delta^{(l)} \) propagates backward from layer to layer via matrix multiplication and element-wise derivative scaling.

Computational cost. The backward pass requires roughly 2x the FLOPs of the forward pass: the forward pass computes activations, and the backward pass computes both the error signals and the weight gradients. Total cost is \( O(|W|) \) per sample, linear in the number of parameters, making it tractable even for networks with billions of weights.

Vanishing and exploding gradients. The recursive multiplication \( (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \) means gradients pass through \( L \) matrix multiplications. If the spectral norms \( \|W^{(l)}\| \) are consistently less than 1, gradients shrink exponentially with depth (vanishing); if greater than 1, they grow exponentially (exploding). This fundamental instability motivated key architectural innovations:

  • ReLU activations — \( \sigma'(z) \in \{0, 1\} \), avoiding the saturation regions of sigmoid/tanh where \( \sigma'(z) \approx 0 \).
  • Residual connections (He et al., 2015) — provide gradient highways that bypass layers.
  • Layer/batch normalization — stabilize the distribution of activations and gradients.
  • Gradient clipping — caps gradient norms to prevent explosions, especially in RNNs.

Historical context. Though the chain rule is centuries old, its systematic application to neural networks was popularized by Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams (1986), who demonstrated that backprop could learn useful internal representations in multi-layer networks — settling a decade-long debate about whether hidden layers could be trained effectively. Earlier independent formulations by Werbos (1974) and Linnainmaa (1970) had gone largely unnoticed.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Output layer error (cross-entropy + softmax). For cross-entropy loss \( \mathcal{L} = -\sum_k y_k \log \hat{y}_k \) with softmax output \( \hat{y}_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}} \), the error simplifies elegantly:

\[ \delta^{(L)}_k = \hat{y}_k - y_k \]

This clean form arises because the Jacobian of softmax composed with cross-entropy reduces to a simple subtraction, avoiding explicit computation of the full softmax Jacobian.

Bias gradient. Since \( z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)} \), we have \( \frac{\partial z^{(l)}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = I \), so \( \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} \).

sections.title: Python
sections.body:
def backprop(X, y, weights, biases, activations, z_values):
    """Backprop for an L-layer network with ReLU hidden + softmax output."""
    L = len(weights)
    m = X.shape[0]
    grads_w, grads_b = [None]*L, [None]*L

    # Output layer error (softmax + cross-entropy)
    delta = activations[L] - y  # (batch, classes)

    for l in reversed(range(L)):
        grads_w[l] = activations[l].T @ delta / m
        grads_b[l] = delta.mean(axis=0)
        if l > 0:
            delta = (delta @ weights[l].T) * (z_values[l-1] > 0)  # ReLU derivative

    return grads_w, grads_b

title: Forward pass
summary: The forward pass propagates input data through the network layer by layer, applying affine transformations and nonlinear activations to produce an output prediction. It is the inference-time computation and also the first phase of traini…

papers.citation: 1960s+ · Various ·

sections.body:
The forward pass propagates input data through the network layer by layer, applying affine transformations and nonlinear activations to produce an output prediction. It is the inference-time computation and also the first phase of training (before the backward pass).

Layer-by-layer computation. For a fully connected network with \( L \) layers, the forward pass computes:

\[ z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}, \qquad a^{(l)} = \sigma(z^{(l)}) \]

where \( a^{(0)} = x \) is the input, \( W^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l \times n_{l-1}} \) is the weight matrix, \( b^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l} \) is the bias vector, and \( \sigma \) is a nonlinear activation function. The final output is \( \hat{y} = a^{(L)} \), possibly passed through a task-specific function (softmax for classification, identity for regression).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Alternating affine transformations (weights) and nonlinearities (activations).

Computational graph. Modern deep learning frameworks (PyTorch, JAX) record every operation during the forward pass into a directed acyclic graph (DAG). Each node stores the operation performed and pointers to its inputs. This graph is then traversed in reverse during the backward pass to compute gradients via automatic differentiation. In PyTorch's eager mode, this graph is built dynamically on each forward call, enabling data-dependent control flow (if/else, variable-length loops). TensorFlow 1.x used static graphs defined before execution.

Cost. For a fully connected layer mapping \( n_{\text{in}} \) inputs to \( n_{\text{out}} \) outputs with batch size \( B \), the forward pass requires \( O(B \cdot n_{\text{in}} \cdot n_{\text{out}}) \) multiply-add operations — dominated by the matrix multiplication \( W a \). For convolutional layers, the cost depends on kernel size, spatial dimensions, and channel counts. The total forward pass cost is linear in the number of parameters, \( O(|W|) \), per sample.

sections.title: Forward pass in different architectures
sections.body:
Architecture Forward pass structure
MLPSequential: \( x \to h_1 \to h_2 \to \cdots \to \hat{y} \)
CNNConv → activation → pool, repeated; then FC head
ResNetResidual blocks: \( a^{(l)} = \sigma(f(a^{(l-1)}) + a^{(l-1)}) \)
TransformerSelf-attention → add & norm → FFN → add & norm, repeated

Training vs. inference. During training, intermediate activations \( a^{(l)} \) must be cached for the backward pass, which dominates memory usage (memory is \( O(L \cdot B \cdot n) \)). At inference time, these intermediates can be discarded layer by layer, reducing memory to \( O(\max_l n_l) \). Gradient checkpointing (Chen et al., 2016) trades compute for memory by recomputing activations during the backward pass instead of storing them.

Python 
import numpy as np

def forward_pass(X, weights, biases, activation=np.maximum):
    """Forward pass for an L-layer network with ReLU activations.

    Returns activations and pre-activations for use in backprop.
    """
    a = X
    activations = [a]
    z_values = []

    for l in range(len(weights) - 1):
        z = a @ weights[l] + biases[l]
        a = activation(0, z)  # ReLU
        z_values.append(z)
        activations.append(a)

    # Output layer (no activation — handled by loss function)
    z = a @ weights[-1] + biases[-1]
    z_values.append(z)
    activations.append(z)

    return activations, z_values

title: Backward pass
summary: The backward pass traverses the computational graph in reverse topological order, applying the chain rule at each node to compute the gradient of the loss with respect to every parameter. It is the second phase of a training step, follow…

papers.citation: 1970 · Seppo Linnainmaa · The Representation of the Cumulative Rounding Error of an Algorithm as a Taylor Expansion of the Local Rounding Errors

sections.body:
The backward pass traverses the computational graph in reverse topological order, applying the chain rule at each node to compute the gradient of the loss with respect to every parameter. It is the second phase of a training step, following the forward pass.

Relationship to the forward pass. The forward pass builds a computational graph and caches intermediate values (activations, pre-activations). The backward pass consumes this graph: starting from the scalar loss \( \mathcal{L} \), it propagates gradient information backward through each operation. Every node in the graph has a local Jacobian, and the backward pass multiplies these Jacobians together via the chain rule to produce the end-to-end gradient.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: A single graph node receives the upstream gradient, multiplies it by the local gradients \( \frac{\partial c}{\partial a} \) and \( \frac{\partial c}{\partial b} \), and passes them backward.

Mechanics at a single node. Consider a node computing \( c = f(a, b) \). Given the upstream gradient \( \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial c} \) (already computed), the backward pass computes:

\[ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial c} \cdot \frac{\partial c}{\partial a}, \qquad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial c} \cdot \frac{\partial c}{\partial b} \]

The local derivatives \( \frac{\partial c}{\partial a} \) and \( \frac{\partial c}{\partial b} \) are specific to the operation \( f \) and are computed analytically. This is repeated for every node in reverse topological order.

sections.title: Common local gradients
sections.body:
Operation Forward Local gradient
Addition: \( c = a + b \)\( c = a + b \)\( \frac{\partial c}{\partial a} = 1, \; \frac{\partial c}{\partial b} = 1 \)
Multiplication: \( c = a \cdot b \)\( c = ab \)\( \frac{\partial c}{\partial a} = b, \; \frac{\partial c}{\partial b} = a \)
MatMul: \( C = AB \)\( C = AB \)\( \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial A} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial C} B^T \)
ReLU: \( c = \max(0, a) \)\( c = \max(0,a) \)\( \frac{\partial c}{\partial a} = \mathbf{1}[a > 0] \)
Sigmoid: \( c = \sigma(a) \)\( c = \frac{1}{1+e^{-a}} \)\( \frac{\partial c}{\partial a} = c(1-c) \)

Computational cost. The backward pass costs approximately 2x the forward pass FLOPs. The forward pass performs one matrix multiply per layer; the backward pass performs two (one for the error signal, one for the weight gradient). Combined, a full training step (forward + backward) costs roughly 3x a single forward pass.

Memory requirements. The backward pass requires access to the activations cached during the forward pass. For a model with \( L \) layers, batch size \( B \), and hidden dimension \( n \), this costs \( O(L \cdot B \cdot n) \) memory. Gradient checkpointing reduces this to \( O(\sqrt{L} \cdot B \cdot n) \) by recomputing activations from checkpoints during the backward pass, at the cost of one additional forward pass.

title: Chain rule
summary: The chain rule is the calculus theorem that gives the derivative of a composition of functions. It is the mathematical foundation of backpropagation: every gradient computation in a neural network is an application of the chain rule.

papers.citation: 1676 · Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz ·

sections.body:
The chain rule is the calculus theorem that gives the derivative of a composition of functions. It is the mathematical foundation of backpropagation: every gradient computation in a neural network is an application of the chain rule.

Single-variable form. If \( y = f(g(x)) \), then:

\[ \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{df}{dg} \cdot \frac{dg}{dx} = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x) \]

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The derivative of a composite function is the product of the derivatives of its parts: \( \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{dy}{du} \cdot \frac{du}{dx} \)

Multivariable form. If \( \mathcal{L} \) depends on \( z \) through multiple intermediate variables \( a_1, \ldots, a_k \), the total derivative sums over all paths:

\[ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a_i} \cdot \frac{\partial a_i}{\partial z} \]

This summation-over-paths form is critical in neural networks, where a single parameter can influence the loss through multiple downstream nodes (e.g., a shared weight in a recurrent network or a parameter used in multiple branches of a residual block).

Application to deep networks. A feedforward network of \( L \) layers computes \( \hat{y} = f_L(f_{L-1}(\cdots f_1(x))) \). The gradient of the loss with respect to the input to layer \( l \) involves a product of \( L - l \) Jacobians:

\[ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(L)}} \prod_{k=l+1}^{L} \frac{\partial z^{(k)}}{\partial z^{(k-1)}} \]

Each factor \( \frac{\partial z^{(k)}}{\partial z^{(k-1)}} \) is the Jacobian of layer \( k \). This product structure directly causes the vanishing/exploding gradient problem: if all Jacobian spectral norms are below 1, the product shrinks exponentially; if above 1, it grows exponentially. The depth \( L \) acts as an exponent on this instability.

Forward vs. reverse mode. The chain rule can be evaluated in two directions:

Mode Direction Cost Best for
Forward (tangent)Input → output\( O(n_{\text{in}}) \) passesFew inputs, many outputs (e.g., Jacobian-vector products)
Reverse (adjoint)Output → input\( O(n_{\text{out}}) \) passesOne scalar output, many inputs (neural network training)

Neural network training has one scalar output (the loss) and millions of inputs (the parameters), so reverse mode (backpropagation) computes all gradients in a single backward pass. Forward mode would require one pass per parameter — completely intractable.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Concrete example: 2-layer network. Let \( \hat{y} = \sigma_2(W_2 \cdot \sigma_1(W_1 x + b_1) + b_2) \) with loss \( \mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(\hat{y} - y)^2 \). The gradient with respect to \( W_1 \) requires chaining through three functions:

\[ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_1} = \underbrace{(\hat{y} - y)}_{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}}} \cdot \underbrace{\sigma_2'(z_2)}_{\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z_2}} \cdot \underbrace{W_2^T}_{\frac{\partial z_2}{\partial a_1}} \cdot \underbrace{\sigma_1'(z_1)}_{\frac{\partial a_1}{\partial z_1}} \cdot \underbrace{x^T}_{\frac{\partial z_1}{\partial W_1}} \]

Each factor is a local derivative at one node, and the full gradient is their product — precisely the chain rule applied recursively.

title: Automatic differentiation
summary: Automatic differentiation (autodiff) computes exact, numerically stable derivatives of functions expressed as computer programs by systematically applying the chain rule to elementary operations. It is the computational backbone of every…

papers.citation: 1970 · Seppo Linnainmaa · The Representation of the Cumulative Rounding Error of an Algorithm as a Taylor Expansion of the Local Rounding Errors

sections.body:
Automatic differentiation (autodiff) computes exact, numerically stable derivatives of functions expressed as computer programs by systematically applying the chain rule to elementary operations. It is the computational backbone of every modern deep learning framework.

Three approaches to computing derivatives:

Method Accuracy Cost Drawback
Numerical (finite differences)\( O(\epsilon) \) or \( O(\epsilon^2) \)\( O(n) \) evaluations for \( n \) paramsTruncation + round-off error; intractable for large \( n \)
Symbolic (CAS)Exact (analytical)VariesExpression swell — derivatives can be exponentially larger than the original expression
AutomaticMachine precision\( O(1) \) passes (reverse mode)Requires storing the computational graph (memory)

sections.title: Illustration

sections.body: How it works. Any program, no matter how complex, is ultimately a composition of elementary operations (+, ×, exp, log, sin, etc.). Autodiff decomposes the program into a computational graph (a DAG of these primitives) and applies the chain rule mechanically at each node. Because each elementary operation has a known, simple derivative rule, the result is exact to machine precision — no approximation.

Forward mode (tangent mode). Propagates directional derivatives from inputs to outputs. For each input perturbation \( \dot{x} \), computes \( \dot{y} = \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} \dot{x} \) alongside the primal computation. Cost: one forward pass per input variable. Efficient when \( n_{\text{in}} \ll n_{\text{out}} \).

Reverse mode (adjoint mode). Propagates adjoint (gradient) information from outputs to inputs. One reverse pass computes the gradient of a scalar output with respect to all inputs simultaneously. Cost: one forward pass + one backward pass, regardless of the number of parameters. This is why reverse mode is universally used for neural network training: a single backward pass produces all \( \sim 10^6 \text{--} 10^{11} \) parameter gradients.

sections.title: Implementation strategies
sections.body:
• Operator overloading (PyTorch, JAX): Overloads arithmetic operators on tensor objects to record operations into a tape/graph during the forward pass. The backward() call replays the tape in reverse. Supports dynamic, data-dependent control flow naturally.
  • Source transformation (Theano, TF 1.x, Tapenade): Transforms the source code or computation graph into a new program that computes both the primal and derivative. More amenable to whole-program optimization but less flexible.

Dual numbers. Forward-mode autodiff can be elegantly implemented using dual numbers \( a + b\epsilon \) where \( \epsilon^2 = 0 \). Evaluating \( f(x + \epsilon) \) produces \( f(x) + f'(x)\epsilon \), extracting the derivative without numerical approximation. JAX's jvp uses this principle.

Higher-order derivatives. Autodiff composes: applying reverse mode to a function that itself uses reverse mode yields second derivatives (Hessian-vector products). This enables second-order optimization methods (e.g., natural gradient, K-FAC) and is used in meta-learning (MAML) where gradients flow through the optimization process itself.

title: Computational graph
summary: A directed acyclic graph (DAG) where nodes represent mathematical operations or variables, and edges represent the flow of data (tensors). It forms the structural basis for automatic differentiation in modern deep learning frameworks.

papers.citation: 1970 · Linnainmaa · The representation of the cumulative rounding error of an algorithm as a Taylor expansion of the local rounding errors

sections.body:
A directed acyclic graph (DAG) where nodes represent mathematical operations or variables, and edges represent the flow of data (tensors). It forms the structural basis for automatic differentiation in modern deep learning frameworks.
